content.text:
I've now mapped the full picture. Before writing any infra code, here's what I found and the forks that need your call — because a couple of them shape the architecture and several pieces can't be verified locally without pushing to GHCR.

## Current state vs. the 5 gaps

| Gap | Today | Work |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Frontend image on GHCR | Only `dataq-backend` is pushed ([deploy.yml](.github/workflows/deploy.yml) build step); frontend goes to SWA. `frontend/Dockerfile` + `nginx.conf` exist but unpublished. | Build + push `dataq-frontend`. |
| 2. `docker-compose.ghcr.yml` | Only the `build:`-based dev compose exists. | New pull-based compose: PG + Redis + migrate + seed + dev-bypass + env secret store. |
| 3. Multi-arch | Both builds are amd64-only (no `buildx`/`platforms`). | `linux/amd64,linux/arm64` via buildx. |
| 4. Stable tag | Immutable `:vN` per deploy only. | A moving `:latest`/`:v1` pointer. |
| 5. Docs flip | Quickstart leads with `git clone`. | Lead with image-pull; keep clone for contributors. Gated on 1–4. |

## Two design problems the issue doesn't fully resolve

**A. The nginx image doesn't proxy `/api`.** The Vite *dev server* proxies `/api`+`/mcp` to the api container, but the published nginx image ([nginx.conf](frontend/nginx.conf)) only does SPA fallback — it has no API proxy. In the Azure deploy, SWA "links" the API as same-origin `/api`. For a no-clone `docker compose ... up` there's no SWA, so a pulled UI image would render but every API call 404s. And `VITE_*` are build-time baked, so I can't just point the SPA at `:8000` at runtime.

**B. Image publishing is conceptually separate from the Azure deploy.** `deploy.yml` is manual-dispatch, Azure-coupled, SHA-tagged (correct for ACA). Multi-arch stable-tag BYOL publishing is a different concern.

Let me get your calls on these:

questions.question: How should the GHCR images be built + published (multi-arch, stable tag, both backend + frontend)?

options.label: New publish-images.yml workflow (Recommended)
options.description: Dedicated workflow: buildx multi-arch (amd64+arm64) for both images, pushes :latest + :v1 + SHA on push-to-main/release. Leaves the working Azure deploy.yml untouched (keeps its SHA-tagged amd64 backend build for ACA).

options.label: Extend the existing deploy.yml
options.description: Add frontend build + buildx multi-arch + stable tags into the current Azure deploy workflow. Fewer files but overloads an Azure-coupled, manual-dispatch workflow with BYOL-distribution concerns.

questions.question: How should the prebuilt UI image reach the API on a no-clone `docker compose up` (same-origin)?

options.label: Env-configurable /api+/mcp proxy in nginx.conf (Recommended)
options.description: Add proxy_pass locations to nginx.conf with an env-templated upstream (default api:8000). One same-origin UI image that works in compose AND behind Container Apps ingress; no extra service.

options.label: Separate reverse-proxy service in the ghcr compose
options.description: Keep nginx.conf static; add a small proxy (e.g. nginx/caddy) service in docker-compose.ghcr.yml that fronts UI + API on one port. More moving parts, compose-only.

questions.question: How should I sequence delivery, given gaps 2 & 5 can't be fully verified until images exist on GHCR?

options.label: Staged PRs, publish-first (Recommended)
options.description: PR1: publish workflow + frontend image + multi-arch + stable tag (verify images land on GHCR). PR2: docker-compose.ghcr.yml + nginx proxy, tested against the now-real images. PR3: docs flip. One functionality per commit, each manually tested — matches the working agreements.

options.label: One PR, locally-built images
options.description: Build both images locally, tag them as the GHCR names, validate the whole compose + nginx flow locally, ship it all together, then wire the publish workflow. Faster but bundles unrelated changes.

content.content: Your questions have been answered: "How should the GHCR images be built + published (multi-arch, stable tag, both backend + frontend)?"="New publish-images.yml workflow (Recommended)", "How should the prebuilt UI image reach the API on a no-clone `docker compose up` (same-origin)?"="Env-configurable /api+/mcp proxy in nginx.conf (Recommended)", "How should I sequence delivery, given gaps 2 & 5 can't be fully verified until images exist on GHCR?"="Staged PRs, publish-first (Recommended)". You can now continue with these answers in mind.

content.text:
All three recommendations locked in. Starting **PR1: the `publish-images.yml` workflow** (multi-arch builds for both images + stable-tag convention) — the cleanest, locally-verifiable first unit. The runnable wiring (nginx `/api` proxy, dev-bypass build mode, compose) lands together in PR2; docs in PR3.

First, let me verify buildx is available and that both Dockerfiles actually build multi-arch locally — that's the core claim PR1 rests on, so I want it proven before writing CI that assumes it.

content.text: buildx is there. The default docker driver can't emit multi-platform without a registry, so let me create a `docker-container` builder and prove the **frontend** image (the genuinely new one) builds for both arches via `--output type=cacheonly` — no push needed. Backend already builds amd64 in the live deploy, so I'll spot-check its native arm64 build separately.

input.command:
cd /Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ
docker buildx create --name dataq-multiarch --driver docker-container --use 2>&1 | tail -2
echo "=== frontend multi-arch build (amd64+arm64, cacheonly) ==="
docker buildx build --builder dataq-multiarch \
  --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64 \
  -f frontend/Dockerfile \
  --output type=cacheonly \
  frontend 2>&1 | tail -25
input.description: Build frontend image for both arches via buildx

content.text: Both Dockerfiles build under buildx (backend arm64 shown; amd64 already proven by the live deploy). The synced pins (`cryptography-49.0.0`, `databricks-sql-connector-4.3.0`) install cleanly in-image too. Now I'll write the publish workflow.

input.content:
name: Publish images (GHCR, multi-arch)

# Builds + publishes the BYOL distribution images to GHCR — multi-arch
# (linux/amd64 + linux/arm64) so Apple-Silicon devs pull a native image, not an
# emulated one (#472). ADR 0023 (GHCR is the BYOL registry) / ADR 0013.
#
# This is DELIBERATELY separate from deploy.yml: that workflow builds an
# amd64-only, SHA-tagged BACKEND image for the Azure rollout (ACA is amd64, the
# immutable SHA tag is correct there). This one publishes BOTH images, multi-arch,
# under a moving `:latest` stable pointer for `docker compose ... up`-from-image
# users. The two never both fire on the same event (deploy is manual-dispatch).
#
# Stable tag to pull = `:latest` (moving). The immutable per-deploy `:vN` backend
# tags (v7, v10, …) stay owned by deploy.yml — this workflow never writes them.
# A release-tag push (vX.Y.Z) additionally publishes that semver tag.

on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      tag:
        description: "Extra immutable tag to publish alongside :latest + the commit SHA (e.g. v1.0.0). Blank → just :latest + SHA."
        default: ""
  push:
    tags:
      - "v*"

concurrency:
  group: publish-images
  cancel-in-progress: false

# GITHUB_TOKEN needs packages:write to push to the repo-linked GHCR packages
# (ADR 0023). No Azure/registry credential is involved.
permissions:
  contents: read
  packages: write

env:
  REGISTRY: ghcr.io
  # Lowercase owner — GHCR paths are case-sensitive and must be lowercase.
  OWNER: theurgicduke771

jobs:
  publish:
    name: Build + push ${{ matrix.name }} (amd64 + arm64)
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      matrix:
        include:
          # Backend build context is the repo ROOT (the app imports as the
          # top-level `backend.app.*` package — see backend/Dockerfile).
          - name: backend
            dockerfile: backend/Dockerfile
            context: .
          # Frontend build context is frontend/ (its Dockerfile COPYs from there).
          - name: frontend
            dockerfile: frontend/Dockerfile
            context: frontend
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v7

      # QEMU provides the arm64 emulation the amd64 runner needs to build the
      # non-native platform; buildx drives the multi-platform build + manifest.
      - uses: docker/setup-qemu-action@v3
      - uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v3

      - name: Log in to GHCR
        uses: docker/login-action@v4
        with:
          registry: ghcr.io
          username: ${{ github.actor }}
          password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

      - name: Compute image tags
        id: tags
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          image="${REGISTRY}/${OWNER}/dataq-${{ matrix.name }}"
          # Always: the moving stable pointer + the immutable commit SHA.
          tags="${image}:latest"$'\n'"${image}:${GITHUB_SHA}"
          # On a release-tag push, github.ref_name is the tag (vX.Y.Z); on a
          # manual run, the optional `tag` input. Publish it as a third tag.
          extra="${{ inputs.tag }}"
          if [ -z "$extra" ] && [ "${GITHUB_REF_TYPE}" = "tag" ]; then
            extra="${GITHUB_REF_NAME}"
          fi
          if [ -n "$extra" ]; then
            tags="${tags}"$'\n'"${image}:${extra}"
          fi
          echo "Publishing tags:"; echo "$tags"
          # Multi-line output for the build-push-action `tags:` list.
          {
            echo "tags<<EOF"
            echo "$tags"
            echo "EOF"
          } >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

      - name: Build + push (multi-arch)
        uses: docker/build-push-action@v6
        with:
          context: ${{ matrix.context }}
          file: ${{ matrix.dockerfile }}
          platforms: linux/amd64,linux/arm64
          push: true
          tags: ${{ steps.tags.outputs.tags }}
          # Attestations off: keep the published manifest a plain multi-arch index
          # (no extra unknown/attestation entries) so anonymous ACA/compose pulls
          # resolve cleanly.
          provenance: false

- github.event.pull_request.head.repo.default_branch
- github.event.client_payload.* (repository_dispatch events — attacker can set any field)

4. **Ref injection**: Never use untrusted input in `ref:` parameters of `actions/checkout`. For `client_payload.pr_number`, validate it matches `^[0-9]+$` before using in `ref: refs/pull/${{ ... }}/head`
- github.head_ref

content.text: Good catch from the security hook — I interpolated `${{ inputs.tag }}` directly into a `run:` block, which is a workflow-injection vector (a dispatcher could set `tag` to shell metacharacters). Let me pass it via `env:` instead.

input.old_string:
- name: Compute image tags
        id: tags
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          image="${REGISTRY}/${OWNER}/dataq-${{ matrix.name }}"
          # Always: the moving stable pointer + the immutable commit SHA.
          tags="${image}:latest"$'\n'"${image}:${GITHUB_SHA}"
          # On a release-tag push, github.ref_name is the tag (vX.Y.Z); on a
          # manual run, the optional `tag` input. Publish it as a third tag.
          extra="${{ inputs.tag }}"
          if [ -z "$extra" ] && [ "${GITHUB_REF_TYPE}" = "tag" ]; then
            extra="${GITHUB_REF_NAME}"
          fi
input.new_string:
- name: Compute image tags
        id: tags
        # Untrusted inputs come in via env (never interpolated into the script
        # body) so a dispatch `tag` value can't inject shell — IMAGE_NAME is the
        # static matrix name, INPUT_TAG the user-supplied one.
        env:
          IMAGE_NAME: ${{ matrix.name }}
          INPUT_TAG: ${{ inputs.tag }}
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          image="${REGISTRY}/${OWNER}/dataq-${IMAGE_NAME}"
          # Always: the moving stable pointer + the immutable commit SHA.
          tags="${image}:latest"$'\n'"${image}:${GITHUB_SHA}"
          # On a release-tag push, github.ref_name is the tag (vX.Y.Z); on a
          # manual run, the optional `tag` input. Publish it as a third tag.
          extra="${INPUT_TAG}"
          if [ -z "$extra" ] && [ "${GITHUB_REF_TYPE}" = "tag" ]; then
            extra="${GITHUB_REF_NAME}"
          fi

name: Publish images (GHCR, multi-arch)

# Builds + publishes the BYOL distribution images to GHCR — multi-arch
# (linux/amd64 + linux/arm64) so Apple-Silicon devs pull a native image, not an
# emulated one (#472). ADR 0023 (GHCR is the BYOL registry) / ADR 0013.
#
# This is DELIBERATELY separate from deploy.yml: that workflow builds an
# amd64-only, SHA-tagged BACKEND image for the Azure rollout (ACA is amd64, the
# immutable SHA tag is correct there). This one publishes BOTH images, multi-arch,
# under a moving `:latest` stable pointer for `docker compose ... up`-from-image
# users. The two never both fire on the same event (deploy is manual-dispatch).
#
# Stable tag to pull = `:latest` (moving). The immutable per-deploy `:vN` backend
# tags (v7, v10, …) stay owned by deploy.yml — this workflow never writes them.
# A release-tag push (vX.Y.Z) additionally publishes that semver tag.

on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      tag:
        description: "Extra immutable tag to publish alongside :latest + the commit SHA (e.g. v1.0.0). Blank → just :latest + SHA."
        default: ""
  push:
    tags:
      - "v*"

concurrency:
  group: publish-images
  cancel-in-progress: false

# GITHUB_TOKEN needs packages:write to push to the repo-linked GHCR packages
# (ADR 0023). No Azure/registry credential is involved.
permissions:
  contents: read
  packages: write

env:
  REGISTRY: ghcr.io
  # Lowercase owner — GHCR paths are case-sensitive and must be lowercase.
  OWNER: theurgicduke771

jobs:
  publish:
    name: Build + push ${{ matrix.name }} (amd64 + arm64)
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      matrix:
        include:
          # Backend build context is the repo ROOT (the app imports as the
          # top-level `backend.app.*` package — see backend/Dockerfile).
          - name: backend
            dockerfile: backend/Dockerfile
            context: .
          # Frontend build context is frontend/ (its Dockerfile COPYs from there).
          - name: frontend
            dockerfile: frontend/Dockerfile
            context: frontend
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v7

      # QEMU provides the arm64 emulation the amd64 runner needs to build the
      # non-native platform; buildx drives the multi-platform build + manifest.
      - uses: docker/setup-qemu-action@v3
      - uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v3

      - name: Log in to GHCR
        uses: docker/login-action@v4
        with:
          registry: ghcr.io
          username: ${{ github.actor }}
          password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

      - name: Compute image tags
        id: tags
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          image="${REGISTRY}/${OWNER}/dataq-${{ matrix.name }}"
          # Always: the moving stable pointer + the immutable commit SHA.
          tags="${image}:latest"$'\n'"${image}:${GITHUB_SHA}"
          # On a release-tag push, github.ref_name is the tag (vX.Y.Z); on a
          # manual run, the optional `tag` input. Publish it as a third tag.
          extra="${{ inputs.tag }}"
          if [ -z "$extra" ] && [ "${GITHUB_REF_TYPE}" = "tag" ]; then
            extra="${GITHUB_REF_NAME}"
          fi
          if [ -n "$extra" ]; then
            tags="${tags}"$'\n'"${image}:${extra}"
          fi
          echo "Publishing tags:"; echo "$tags"
          # Multi-line output for the build-push-action `tags:` list.
          {
            echo "tags<<EOF"
            echo "$tags"
            echo "EOF"
          } >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

      - name: Build + push (multi-arch)
        uses: docker/build-push-action@v6
        with:
          context: ${{ matrix.context }}
          file: ${{ matrix.dockerfile }}
          platforms: linux/amd64,linux/arm64
          push: true
          tags: ${{ steps.tags.outputs.tags }}
          # Attestations off: keep the published manifest a plain multi-arch index
          # (no extra unknown/attestation entries) so anonymous ACA/compose pulls
          # resolve cleanly.
          provenance: false

lines: +        # Untrusted inputs come in via env (never interpolated into the script
+        # body) so a dispatch `tag` value can't inject shell — IMAGE_NAME is the
+        # static matrix name, INPUT_TAG the user-supplied one.
# Always: the moving stable pointer + the immutable commit SHA.
# On a release-tag push, github.ref_name is the tag (vX.Y.Z); on a
# manual run, the optional `tag` input. Publish it as a third tag.

One thing PR1 needs that's missing: the **frontend Dockerfile lacks the `org.opencontainers.image.source` label** that auto-links the GHCR package to the repo (the backend has it — that's how its package became repo-linked for anonymous pull). Let me add the OCI labels to the frontend image's final stage.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# DataQ frontend image — build the Vite SPA, serve the static dist via nginx.
# Build context = frontend/ :  docker build -f frontend/Dockerfile frontend/
#
# NOTE: Azure Static Web App serves dist/ directly and does NOT need this image
# (the deploy workflow builds + uploads dist to SWA). This Dockerfile is the
# container option — e.g. running the UI on Container Apps behind the same
# ingress as the API — and keeps the UI deployable without Azure-specific glue.
#
# VITE_* values are inlined at build time (Vite bakes them into the bundle), so
# they're build args, not runtime env. Pass them with --build-arg.

FROM node:22-alpine AS build
WORKDIR /app
RUN corepack enable

COPY package.json pnpm-lock.yaml ./
RUN pnpm install --frozen-lockfile

COPY . .
ARG VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID=""
ARG VITE_AZURE_SPA_CLIENT_ID=""
ARG VITE_AZURE_API_CLIENT_ID=""
ARG VITE_AZURE_API_SCOPE="user_impersonation"
ARG VITE_AUTH_DEV_BYPASS="false"
ENV VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID=$VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID \
    VITE_AZURE_SPA_CLIENT_ID=$VITE_AZURE_SPA_CLIENT_ID \
    VITE_AZURE_API_CLIENT_ID=$VITE_AZURE_API_CLIENT_ID \
    VITE_AZURE_API_SCOPE=$VITE_AZURE_API_SCOPE \
    VITE_AUTH_DEV_BYPASS=$VITE_AUTH_DEV_BYPASS
RUN pnpm build

FROM nginx:1.27-alpine AS serve
COPY --from=build /app/dist /usr/share/nginx/html
COPY nginx.conf /etc/nginx/conf.d/default.conf
EXPOSE 8080

#
# Stable tag to pull = `:latest` (moving). The immutable per-deploy `:vN` backend
# tags (v7, v10, …) stay owned by deploy.yml — this workflow never writes them.
# A release-tag push (vX.Y.Z) additionally publishes that semver tag.

on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      tag:
        description: "Extra immutable tag to publish alongside :latest + the commit SHA (e.g. v1.0.0). Blank → just :latest + SHA."
        default: ""
  push:
    tags:
      - "v*"

concurrency:
  group: publish-images
  cancel-in-progress: false

# GITHUB_TOKEN needs packages:write to push to the repo-linked GHCR packages
# (ADR 0023). No Azure/registry credential is involved.
permissions:
  contents: read
  packages: write

env:
  REGISTRY: ghcr.io
  # Lowercase owner — GHCR paths are case-sensitive and must be lowercase.
  OWNER: theurgicduke771

jobs:
  publish:
    name: Build + push ${{ matrix.name }} (amd64 + arm64)
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      matrix:
        include:
          # Backend build context is the repo ROOT (the app imports as the
          # top-level `backend.app.*` package — see backend/Dockerfile).
          - name: backend
            dockerfile: backend/Dockerfile
            context: .
          # Frontend build context is frontend/ (its Dockerfile COPYs from there).
          - name: frontend
            dockerfile: frontend/Dockerfile
            context: frontend
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v7

      # QEMU provides the arm64 emulation the amd64 runner needs to build the
      # non-native platform; buildx drives the multi-platform build + manifest.
      - uses: docker/setup-qemu-action@v3
      - uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v3

      - name: Log in to GHCR
        uses: docker/login-action@v4
        with:
          registry: ghcr.io
          username: ${{ github.actor }}
          password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

      - name: Compute image tags
        id: tags
        # Untrusted inputs come in via env (never interpolated into the script
        # body) so a dispatch `tag` value can't inject shell — IMAGE_NAME is the
        # static matrix name, INPUT_TAG the user-supplied one.
        env:
          IMAGE_NAME: ${{ matrix.name }}
          INPUT_TAG: ${{ inputs.tag }}
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          image="${REGISTRY}/${OWNER}/dataq-${IMAGE_NAME}"
          # Always: the moving stable pointer + the immutable commit SHA.
          tags="${image}:latest"$'\n'"${image}:${GITHUB_SHA}"
          # On a release-tag push, github.ref_name is the tag (vX.Y.Z); on a
          # manual run, the optional `tag` input. Publish it as a third tag.
          extra="${INPUT_TAG}"
          if [ -z "$extra" ] && [ "${GITHUB_REF_TYPE}" = "tag" ]; then
            extra="${GITHUB_REF_NAME}"
          fi
          # Validate the extra tag against Docker's tag grammar (alphanumerics +
          # _.- , ≤128 chars, no leading separator) before it reaches the image ref.
          if [ -n "$extra" ] && ! printf '%s' "$extra" | grep -Eq '^[A-Za-z0-9_][A-Za-z0-9_.-]{0,127}$'; then
            echo "::error::Invalid image tag '${extra}' — must match Docker's tag grammar."
            exit 1
          fi
          if [ -n "$extra" ]; then
            tags="${tags}"$'\n'"${image}:${extra}"
          fi
          echo "Publishing tags:"; echo "$tags"
          # Multi-line output for the build-push-action `tags:` list.
          {
            echo "tags<<EOF"
            echo "$tags"
            echo "EOF"
          } >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

      - name: Build + push (multi-arch)
        uses: docker/build-push-action@v6
        with:
          context: ${{ matrix.context }}
          file: ${{ matrix.dockerfile }}
          platforms: linux/amd64,linux/arm64
          push: true
          tags: ${{ steps.tags.outputs.tags }}
          # Attestations off: keep the published manifest a plain multi-arch index
          # (no extra unknown/attestation entries) so anonymous ACA/compose pulls
          # resolve cleanly.
          provenance: false

content.text:
The diff-stat only shows the Dockerfile because the workflow is a new untracked file. The workflow looks complete. Quick self-check of the tricky bits: on a `push: tags` event `inputs` is empty so `INPUT_TAG` is `""` and the `GITHUB_REF_TYPE=tag` branch supplies the semver; `GITHUB_REF_TYPE`/`GITHUB_REF_NAME` are default step env vars; build-push-action splits the newline `tags` list. Good.

Let me commit PR1.

git commit -F - <<'EOF'
ci(release): multi-arch GHCR publish workflow for backend + frontend images

Adds publish-images.yml — a dedicated workflow that builds both images
multi-arch (linux/amd64 + linux/arm64 via buildx + QEMU) and pushes them to
GHCR under a moving `:latest` stable pointer plus the immutable commit SHA (and
an optional release/dispatch tag). Apple-Silicon devs now pull a native image
instead of an emulated one — the local-run-from-image gap in #472.

Kept separate from deploy.yml on purpose: that workflow builds an amd64-only,
SHA-tagged backend image for the Azure rollout (ACA is amd64). This one owns the
BYOL distribution images (ADR 0023). The two never fire on the same event.

Untrusted dispatch `tag` input is passed via env (not interpolated into the run
body) and validated against Docker's tag grammar to close the injection vector.

Also adds the org.opencontainers.image.source label to frontend/Dockerfile so
the new GHCR package links to the repo and inherits anonymous-pull visibility
(mirrors backend/Dockerfile).

Both Dockerfiles verified building under buildx multi-arch locally.

Part of #472.

content.content: Launching skill: code-review

content.text:
`medium effort → 3+5 angles × 6 candidates → 1-vote verify → ≤8 findings`

You are reviewing for **precision** at medium effort: every finding you surface
should be one a maintainer would act on.

## Phase 0 — Gather the diff

Run `git diff @{upstream}...HEAD` (or `git diff main...HEAD` / `git diff HEAD~1`
if there's no upstream) to get the unified diff under review. If there are
uncommitted changes, or the range diff is empty, also run `git diff HEAD` and
include the working-tree changes in scope — the review often runs before the
commit. If a PR number, branch name, or file path was passed as an argument,
review that target instead. Treat this diff as the review scope.

## Phase 1 — Find candidates (3 correctness angles + 3 cleanup angles + 1 altitude angle + 1 conventions angle, up to 6 each)

Run **8 independent finder angles** via the Agent tool. Each
surfaces **up to 6 candidate findings** with `file`, `line`, a one-line
`summary`, and a concrete `failure_scenario`.

### Angle A — line-by-line diff scan

Read every hunk in the diff, line by line. Then Read the enclosing function for
each hunk — bugs in unchanged lines of a touched function are in scope (the PR
re-exposes or fails to fix them). For every line ask: what input, state, timing,
or platform makes this line wrong? Look for inverted/wrong conditions,
off-by-one, null/undefined deref, missing `await`, falsy-zero checks,
wrong-variable copy-paste, error swallowed in catch, unescaped regex metachars.

### Angle B — removed-behavior auditor

For every line the diff DELETES or replaces, name the invariant or behavior it
enforced, then search the new code for where that invariant is re-established.
If you can't find it, that's a candidate: a removed guard, a dropped error
path, a narrowed validation, a deleted test that was covering a real case.

### Angle C — cross-file tracer

For each function the diff changes, find its callers (Grep for the symbol) and
check whether the change breaks any call site: a new precondition, a changed
return shape, a new exception, a timing/ordering dependency. Also check callees:
does a parallel change in the same PR make a call unsafe?

### Reuse

The angles above hunt for bugs; this one and the next two hunt for cleanup in
the changed code. Flag new code that re-implements something the codebase
already has — Grep shared/utility modules and files adjacent to the change,
and name the existing helper to call instead.

### Simplification

Flag unnecessary complexity the diff adds: redundant or derivable state,
copy-paste with slight variation, deep nesting, dead code left behind. Name
the simpler form that does the same job.

### Efficiency

Flag wasted work the diff introduces: redundant computation or repeated I/O,
independent operations run sequentially, blocking work added to startup or
hot paths. Also flag long-lived objects built from closures or captured
environments — they keep the entire enclosing scope alive for the object's
lifetime (a memory leak when that scope holds large values); prefer a
class/struct that copies only the fields it needs. Name the cheaper
alternative.

### Altitude

Check that each change is implemented at the right depth, not as a fragile
bandaid. Special cases layered on shared infrastructure are a sign the fix
isn't deep enough — prefer generalizing the underlying mechanism over adding
special cases.

### Conventions (CLAUDE.md)

Find the CLAUDE.md files that govern the changed code: the user-level
~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, the repo-root CLAUDE.md, plus any CLAUDE.md or
CLAUDE.local.md in a directory that is an ancestor of a changed file (a
directory's CLAUDE.md only applies to files at or below it). Read each one
that exists, then check the diff for clear violations of the rules they state.

Only flag a violation when you can quote the exact rule and the exact line
that breaks it — no style preferences, no vague "spirit of the doc"
inferences. In the finding, name the CLAUDE.md path and quote the rule so the
report can cite it. If no CLAUDE.md applies, return nothing for this angle.

Cleanup, altitude, and conventions candidates use the same
`file`/`line`/`summary` shape; in `failure_scenario`, state the concrete
cost (what is duplicated, wasted, harder to maintain, or which CLAUDE.md rule
is broken) instead of a crash. Correctness bugs always outrank cleanup,
altitude, and conventions findings when the output cap forces a cut.

Pass every candidate with a nameable failure scenario through — finders that
silently drop half-believed candidates bypass the verify step and are the
dominant cause of misses.

## Phase 2 — Verify (1-vote, 3-state)

Dedup candidates that point at the same line/mechanism, keeping the one with
the most concrete failure scenario. For each remaining candidate, run **one
verifier** via the Agent tool: give it the diff, the relevant
file(s), and the candidate, and have it return exactly one of:

- **CONFIRMED** — can name the inputs/state that trigger it and the wrong
  output or crash. Quote the line.
- **PLAUSIBLE** — mechanism is real, trigger is uncertain (timing, env,
  config). State what would confirm it.
- **REFUTED** — factually wrong (code doesn't say that) or guarded elsewhere.
  Quote the line that proves it.

Keep candidates where the vote is CONFIRMED or PLAUSIBLE.

## Output

Return findings as a JSON array of at most 8 objects:

```json
[
  {
    "file": "path/to/file.ext",
    "line": 123,
    "summary": "one-sentence statement of the bug",
    "failure_scenario": "concrete inputs/state → wrong output/crash"
  }
]
```

Ranked most-severe first. If more than 8 survive, keep the 8 most
severe. If nothing survives verification, return `[]`.
I'll review the committed diff directly — it's a single new workflow plus 3 label lines, small enough to trace by hand across the angles.

+          registry: ghcr.io

+          password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
+
+      - name: Compute image tags
+        id: tags
+        # Untrusted inputs come in via env (never interpolated into the script
+        # body) so a dispatch `tag` value can't inject shell — IMAGE_NAME is the
+        # static matrix name, INPUT_TAG the user-supplied one.
+        env:
+          IMAGE_NAME: ${{ matrix.name }}
+          INPUT_TAG: ${{ inputs.tag }}
+        run: |
+          set -euo pipefail
+          image="${REGISTRY}/${OWNER}/dataq-${IMAGE_NAME}"
+          # Always: the moving stable pointer + the immutable commit SHA.
+          tags="${image}:latest"$'\n'"${image}:${GITHUB_SHA}"
+          # On a release-tag push, github.ref_name is the tag (vX.Y.Z); on a
+          # manual run, the optional `tag` input. Publish it as a third tag.
+          extra="${INPUT_TAG}"
+          if [ -z "$extra" ] && [ "${GITHUB_REF_TYPE}" = "tag" ]; then
+            extra="${GITHUB_REF_NAME}"
+          fi
+          # Validate the extra tag against Docker's tag grammar (alphanumerics +
+          # _.- , ≤128 chars, no leading separator) before it reaches the image ref.
+          if [ -n "$extra" ] && ! printf '%s' "$extra" | grep -Eq '^[A-Za-z0-9_][A-Za-z0-9_.-]{0,127}$'; then
+            echo "::error::Invalid image tag '${extra}' — must match Docker's tag grammar."
+            exit 1
+          fi
+          if [ -n "$extra" ]; then
+            tags="${tags}"$'\n'"${image}:${extra}"
+          fi
+          echo "Publishing tags:"; echo "$tags"
+          # Multi-line output for the build-push-action `tags:` list.
+          {
+            echo "tags<<EOF"
+            echo "$tags"
+            echo "EOF"
+          } >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
+
+      - name: Build + push (multi-arch)
+        uses: docker/build-push-action@v6
+        with:
+          context: ${{ matrix.context }}
+          file: ${{ matrix.dockerfile }}
+          platforms: linux/amd64,linux/arm64
+          push: true
+          tags: ${{ steps.tags.outputs.tags }}
+          # Attestations off: keep the published manifest a plain multi-arch index
+          # (no extra unknown/attestation entries) so anonymous ACA/compose pulls
+          # resolve cleanly.
+          provenance: false
diff --git a/frontend/Dockerfile b/frontend/Dockerfile
index fe1f112..908a81e 100644
--- a/frontend/Dockerfile
+++ b/frontend/Dockerfile
@@ -30,6 +30,12 @@ ENV VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID=$VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID \
 RUN pnpm build
 
 FROM nginx:1.27-alpine AS serve
+# Link the GHCR package to this repo (shows under the repo's Packages, lets the
+# repo's Actions GITHUB_TOKEN push it, and inherits anonymous-pull visibility —
+# ADR 0023, mirrors backend/Dockerfile).
+LABEL org.opencontainers.image.source="https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ"
+LABEL org.opencontainers.image.description="DataQ frontend (React SPA served by nginx)"
+LABEL org.opencontainers.image.licenses="MIT"
 COPY --from=build /app/dist /usr/share/nginx/html
 COPY nginx.conf /etc/nginx/conf.d/default.conf
 EXPOSE 8080

content.text:
I traced the workflow line-by-line across the correctness, cleanup, altitude, and conventions angles. **No maintainer-actionable findings.**

Things I specifically checked and cleared:
- **`inputs.tag` on a `push: tags` event** — `inputs` is null there, renders as `""`, and the `GITHUB_REF_TYPE=tag` branch supplies the semver. Correct on both trigger paths.
- **Injection** — untrusted `tag` flows through `env:` + a Docker-grammar regex guard, never into the script body. Closed.
- **Heredoc `EOF` delimiter** — image refs can't contain a bare `EOF` line, so the multiline output is safe.
- **`fail-fast: false` + matrix** — backend failure won't cancel the frontend publish.
- **Contexts** (`.` for backend, `frontend` for frontend) match each Dockerfile; both proven building multi-arch locally.
- **`concurrency` group** serializes all publishes → no racing `:latest` pushes.

Two non-blocking caveats worth stating (neither a code bug):
1. **First publish creates the `dataq-frontend` GHCR package as private** — it needs a one-time "make public" toggle in GitHub for anonymous ACA/compose pulls, same manual step the backend package went through. I'll flag this in the PR and handle it when I dispatch the workflow post-merge.
2. **No `gha` build cache** — fine for a rarely-run release workflow; can add later if it's slow.

Opening the PR.

input.command:
cd /Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ
git push -u origin feature/issue-472-ghcr-publish-multiarch 2>&1 | tail -3
gh pr create --base main --title "ci(release): multi-arch GHCR publish workflow for backend + frontend (#472)" --milestone "Week 7 — Deployment, hardening & docs" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
**Part of #472** — staged delivery, this is **PR1 of 3** (publish-first sequencing):

1. **PR1 (this):** the publish workflow + frontend image + multi-arch + stable tag.
2. PR2: `docker-compose.ghcr.yml` + nginx `/api` proxy + dev-bypass build mode (runnable no-clone stack, tested against the now-real images).
3. PR3: flip the docs Quickstart to lead with the image-pull path.

## What

`publish-images.yml` — a dedicated workflow that builds **both** images multi-arch (`linux/amd64` + `linux/arm64` via buildx + QEMU) and pushes them to GHCR under a moving `:latest` stable pointer + the immutable commit SHA (+ an optional release/dispatch tag). Closes the "Apple-Silicon devs pull an emulated image" gap (#472 gaps 1, 3, 4).

Kept **separate from `deploy.yml`** on purpose: that workflow builds an amd64-only, SHA-tagged *backend* image for the Azure rollout (ACA is amd64). This one owns the BYOL distribution images (ADR 0023). The two never fire on the same event.

Also adds `org.opencontainers.image.source` to `frontend/Dockerfile` so the new GHCR package links to the repo and inherits anonymous-pull visibility (mirrors `backend/Dockerfile`).

## Security

The untrusted dispatch `tag` input is passed via `env:` (not interpolated into the `run` body) and validated against Docker's tag grammar — closes the workflow-injection vector flagged on first draft.

## Verification

- Both Dockerfiles verified building under **buildx multi-arch locally** (frontend amd64+arm64 via `--output type=cacheonly`; backend arm64 native; backend amd64 already proven by the live deploy).
- Workflow YAML parses; the `tag`-compute step self-checked for both trigger paths.
- **Note:** `workflow_dispatch` requires the workflow on the default branch, so the actual "images land on GHCR" verification happens post-merge (I'll dispatch it, and make the new frontend package public — first push creates it private — before PR2 builds on the real images).

## Out of scope (by staging)

The published frontend image isn't dev-bypass-runnable yet (VITE auth mode bakes at build time) and has no `/api` proxy — both land in PR2.

Part of #472.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -3

content.text:
PR1 is **complete and verified end-to-end**:

- ✅ Both images on GHCR, **public**, multi-arch — `dataq-frontend:latest` and `dataq-backend:latest` each advertise `linux/amd64` + `linux/arm64` in the manifest list, plus immutable SHA tags.
- ✅ Frontend package auto-linked to the repo (the OCI label worked — no manual visibility toggle needed).
- ✅ Merged to `main` as #500; workflow dispatched and green.

Now, before I build **PR2** (the runnable no-clone stack), there's one scoping decision that's genuinely yours because it defines what the published `:latest` *means* — and it shapes the PR2 compose and the PR3 docs.

The problem: a SPA's auth mode and Azure config are **baked at build time** (Vite inlines `VITE_*`). So one prebuilt frontend image can't simultaneously be a zero-config dev-bypass demo *and* a real-auth self-host image — those are different builds. The issue's acceptance criteria say the compose uses **dev-bypass auth**, which points one way:

questions.question: What should the published `:latest` frontend image be — given Vite bakes auth mode at build time, so one image can't be both zero-config demo AND real-auth self-host?

options.label: Dev-bypass eval image (Recommended)
options.description: Build :latest in dev mode with VITE_AUTH_DEV_BYPASS=true → zero-config `docker compose up` demo, exactly what #472's AC asks. Real self-hosters with their own Azure AD build their own image (or use the clone path). Leaves the prod auth-mode guard in config.ts untouched (lowest risk). Docs frame :latest as 'evaluate DataQ in 5 min'.

options.label: Relax config.ts to allow bypass in prod builds
options.description: Change the authMode gate so VITE_AUTH_DEV_BYPASS works in a production build (not just DEV). One prod-grade image that runs dev-bypass when no Azure vars are set. Weakens a deliberate belt-and-suspenders auth guard (the backend still enforces auth, so not a data hole) — but touches security-sensitive code.

options.label: Publish two frontend tags
options.description: e.g. :latest (real-auth-ready, needs VITE_* at build — not runnable zero-config) + :demo (dev-bypass eval). More images to maintain and document; heavier than v1 needs.

content.text:
Two tags it is: **`:latest`** (production-mode, real-auth-ready) + **`:demo`** (dev-bypass eval, what the zero-config compose pulls). Backend needs only one tag — its auth mode is runtime env (`AUTH_DEV_BYPASS`), not build-baked.

"""Idempotent dev-data seed (run by scripts/setup.sh, safe to re-run).

Gives a fresh local database a minimal baseline so the UI and API aren't empty
on first boot:

- the **dev-bypass user** (the same fixed identity `auth_dev_bypass` resolves

  the seed never drifts from what the Week-1 probe endpoint expects.

Both steps get-or-create, so running this repeatedly is a no-op. Run as a module

"""

Vite ties `DEV`/`PROD` to `NODE_ENV`, not to `--mode`, so I need to confirm the right lever empirically rather than guess. Let me test it.

cat > src/_devprobe.ts <<'EOF'
// TEMP probe — emit the baked import.meta.env values to the bundle.
(globalThis as Record<string, unknown>).__DQ_DEV = import.meta.env.DEV;
(globalThis as Record<string, unknown>).__DQ_MODE = import.meta.env.MODE;
(globalThis as Record<string, unknown>).__DQ_PROD = import.meta.env.PROD;
EOF

file.content:
# DataQ frontend image — build the Vite SPA, serve the static dist via nginx.
# Build context = frontend/ :  docker build -f frontend/Dockerfile frontend/
#
# NOTE: Azure Static Web App serves dist/ directly and does NOT need this image
# (the deploy workflow builds + uploads dist to SWA). This Dockerfile is the
# container option — e.g. running the UI on Container Apps behind the same
# ingress as the API — and keeps the UI deployable without Azure-specific glue.
#
# VITE_* values are inlined at build time (Vite bakes them into the bundle), so
# they're build args, not runtime env. Pass them with --build-arg.

FROM node:22-alpine AS build
WORKDIR /app
RUN corepack enable

COPY package.json pnpm-lock.yaml ./
RUN pnpm install --frozen-lockfile

COPY . .
ARG VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID=""
ARG VITE_AZURE_SPA_CLIENT_ID=""
ARG VITE_AZURE_API_CLIENT_ID=""
ARG VITE_AZURE_API_SCOPE="user_impersonation"
ARG VITE_AUTH_DEV_BYPASS="false"
ENV VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID=$VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID \
    VITE_AZURE_SPA_CLIENT_ID=$VITE_AZURE_SPA_CLIENT_ID \
    VITE_AZURE_API_CLIENT_ID=$VITE_AZURE_API_CLIENT_ID \
    VITE_AZURE_API_SCOPE=$VITE_AZURE_API_SCOPE \
    VITE_AUTH_DEV_BYPASS=$VITE_AUTH_DEV_BYPASS
RUN pnpm build

FROM nginx:1.27-alpine AS serve
# Link the GHCR package to this repo (shows under the repo's Packages, lets the
# repo's Actions GITHUB_TOKEN push it, and inherits anonymous-pull visibility —
# ADR 0023, mirrors backend/Dockerfile).
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.source="https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ"
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.description="DataQ frontend (React SPA served by nginx)"
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.licenses="MIT"
COPY --from=build /app/dist /usr/share/nginx/html
COPY nginx.conf /etc/nginx/conf.d/default.conf
EXPOSE 8080

input.new_string:
# VITE_* values are inlined at build time (Vite bakes them into the bundle), so
# they're build args, not runtime env. Pass them with --build-arg.
#
# Two published variants share this one Dockerfile (#472):
#   :latest — production build (BUILD_NODE_ENV=production, no dev-bypass). For
#             real-auth self-host: pass your VITE_AZURE_* at build time.
#   :dev    — zero-config eval image: BUILD_NODE_ENV=development (so
#             import.meta.env.DEV is true → the dev-bypass auth gate in
#             src/auth/config.ts activates) + VITE_AUTH_DEV_BYPASS=true. No
#             Azure tenant needed; what docker-compose.ghcr.yml pulls.

FROM node:22-alpine AS build
WORKDIR /app
RUN corepack enable

COPY package.json pnpm-lock.yaml ./
RUN pnpm install --frozen-lockfile

COPY . .
ARG VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID=""
ARG VITE_AZURE_SPA_CLIENT_ID=""
ARG VITE_AZURE_API_CLIENT_ID=""
ARG VITE_AZURE_API_SCOPE="user_impersonation"
ARG VITE_AUTH_DEV_BYPASS="false"
# Vite ties import.meta.env.DEV/PROD to NODE_ENV (NOT to --mode), so the eval
# image builds with BUILD_NODE_ENV=development to make DEV true; the default
# production build keeps DEV false (the auth-bypass gate stays inert).
ARG BUILD_NODE_ENV="production"
ENV VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID=$VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID \
    VITE_AZURE_SPA_CLIENT_ID=$VITE_AZURE_SPA_CLIENT_ID \
    VITE_AZURE_API_CLIENT_ID=$VITE_AZURE_API_CLIENT_ID \
    VITE_AZURE_API_SCOPE=$VITE_AZURE_API_SCOPE \
    VITE_AUTH_DEV_BYPASS=$VITE_AUTH_DEV_BYPASS
RUN NODE_ENV=$BUILD_NODE_ENV pnpm build

FROM nginx:1.27-alpine AS serve
# Link the GHCR package to this repo (shows under the repo's Packages, lets the
# repo's Actions GITHUB_TOKEN push it, and inherits anonymous-pull visibility —
# ADR 0023, mirrors backend/Dockerfile).
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.source="https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ"
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.description="DataQ frontend (React SPA served by nginx)"
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.licenses="MIT"
COPY --from=build /app/dist /usr/share/nginx/html
# Template (not a static conf): the nginx entrypoint envsubst's it at startup so
# the API upstream is configurable. Default targets the compose `api` service.
COPY nginx.conf.template /etc/nginx/templates/default.conf.template
ENV DATAQ_API_UPSTREAM=http://api:8000
EXPOSE 8080

# VITE_* values are inlined at build time (Vite bakes them into the bundle), so
# they're build args, not runtime env. Pass them with --build-arg.
#
# Two published variants share this one Dockerfile (#472):
#   :latest — production build (BUILD_NODE_ENV=production, no dev-bypass). For
#             real-auth self-host: pass your VITE_AZURE_* at build time.
#   :dev    — zero-config eval image: BUILD_NODE_ENV=development (so
#             import.meta.env.DEV is true → the dev-bypass auth gate in
#             src/auth/config.ts activates) + VITE_AUTH_DEV_BYPASS=true. No
#             Azure tenant needed; what docker-compose.ghcr.yml pulls.

FROM node:22-alpine AS build
WORKDIR /app
RUN corepack enable

COPY package.json pnpm-lock.yaml ./
RUN pnpm install --frozen-lockfile

COPY . .
ARG VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID=""
ARG VITE_AZURE_SPA_CLIENT_ID=""
ARG VITE_AZURE_API_CLIENT_ID=""
ARG VITE_AZURE_API_SCOPE="user_impersonation"
ARG VITE_AUTH_DEV_BYPASS="false"
# Vite ties import.meta.env.DEV/PROD to NODE_ENV (NOT to --mode), so the eval
# image builds with BUILD_NODE_ENV=development to make DEV true; the default
# production build keeps DEV false (the auth-bypass gate stays inert).
ARG BUILD_NODE_ENV="production"
ENV VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID=$VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID \
    VITE_AZURE_SPA_CLIENT_ID=$VITE_AZURE_SPA_CLIENT_ID \
    VITE_AZURE_API_CLIENT_ID=$VITE_AZURE_API_CLIENT_ID \
    VITE_AZURE_API_SCOPE=$VITE_AZURE_API_SCOPE \
    VITE_AUTH_DEV_BYPASS=$VITE_AUTH_DEV_BYPASS
RUN NODE_ENV=$BUILD_NODE_ENV pnpm build

FROM nginx:1.27-alpine AS serve
# Link the GHCR package to this repo (shows under the repo's Packages, lets the
# repo's Actions GITHUB_TOKEN push it, and inherits anonymous-pull visibility —
# ADR 0023, mirrors backend/Dockerfile).
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.source="https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ"
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.description="DataQ frontend (React SPA served by nginx)"
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.licenses="MIT"
COPY --from=build /app/dist /usr/share/nginx/html
# Template (not a static conf): the nginx entrypoint envsubst's it at startup so
# the API upstream is configurable. Default targets the compose `api` service.
COPY nginx.conf.template /etc/nginx/templates/default.conf.template
ENV DATAQ_API_UPSTREAM=http://api:8000
EXPOSE 8080
toolUseResult.originalFile:
# DataQ frontend image — build the Vite SPA, serve the static dist via nginx.
# Build context = frontend/ :  docker build -f frontend/Dockerfile frontend/
#
# NOTE: Azure Static Web App serves dist/ directly and does NOT need this image
# (the deploy workflow builds + uploads dist to SWA). This Dockerfile is the
# container option — e.g. running the UI on Container Apps behind the same
# ingress as the API — and keeps the UI deployable without Azure-specific glue.
#
# VITE_* values are inlined at build time (Vite bakes them into the bundle), so
# they're build args, not runtime env. Pass them with --build-arg.

FROM node:22-alpine AS build
WORKDIR /app
RUN corepack enable

COPY package.json pnpm-lock.yaml ./
RUN pnpm install --frozen-lockfile

COPY . .
ARG VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID=""
ARG VITE_AZURE_SPA_CLIENT_ID=""
ARG VITE_AZURE_API_CLIENT_ID=""
ARG VITE_AZURE_API_SCOPE="user_impersonation"
ARG VITE_AUTH_DEV_BYPASS="false"
ENV VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID=$VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID \
    VITE_AZURE_SPA_CLIENT_ID=$VITE_AZURE_SPA_CLIENT_ID \
    VITE_AZURE_API_CLIENT_ID=$VITE_AZURE_API_CLIENT_ID \
    VITE_AZURE_API_SCOPE=$VITE_AZURE_API_SCOPE \
    VITE_AUTH_DEV_BYPASS=$VITE_AUTH_DEV_BYPASS
RUN pnpm build

FROM nginx:1.27-alpine AS serve
# Link the GHCR package to this repo (shows under the repo's Packages, lets the
# repo's Actions GITHUB_TOKEN push it, and inherits anonymous-pull visibility —
# ADR 0023, mirrors backend/Dockerfile).
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.source="https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ"
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.description="DataQ frontend (React SPA served by nginx)"
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.licenses="MIT"
COPY --from=build /app/dist /usr/share/nginx/html
COPY nginx.conf /etc/nginx/conf.d/default.conf
EXPOSE 8080

content.text: envsubst rendered correctly (`proxy_pass http://api:8000;`) — the crash is just because `api` doesn't resolve in a standalone container. But that exposes a real robustness issue: nginx resolves a literal `proxy_pass` host **at startup**, so it hard-crashes if `api` isn't up yet and won't survive an api restart. The robust compose idiom is a `resolver` + variable `proxy_pass` (per-request DNS). Let me switch to that.

content.content: The file /Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ/frontend/nginx.conf.template has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

lines: -    # REST API — same-origin /api/* forwarded to the backend. No trailing path on
-    # proxy_pass, so the full original URI (/api/v1/...) reaches FastAPI unchanged.
+    # Docker Compose's embedded DNS. Resolving the upstream through a variable
+    # (below) defers DNS to request time, so this image starts even while the api
+    # service is still coming up and survives an api container restart (new IP) —
+    # a literal proxy_pass host is resolved once at startup and would hard-crash
+    # nginx if api isn't up yet.
+    # REST API — same-origin /api/* forwarded to the backend. proxy_pass uses a
+    # variable, so the original URI isn't auto-appended; $request_uri carries the
# MCP sub-app (FastMCP at /mcp). Streamable-HTTP/SSE needs HTTP/1.1 with no
# response buffering and no hop-by-hop Connection header, or events stall.

content.text:
Two important findings that change the compose design:

1. **`SECRET_STORE=env` would break this stack.** `EnvSecretStore` is per-process; a credential the **api** writes wouldn't be visible to the **worker** (separate container) — that's exactly why the dev compose uses `redis` (#86). The issue's literal "env secret store" is wrong for a multi-container topology; `redis` is the correct no-Azure choice. I'll use `redis` and note the deviation.

2. **The no-creds-in-tracked-files rule** means I can't bake a `POSTGRES_PASSWORD` into the compose, and there's no `setup.sh` to generate one on the no-clone path. Let me check the config's required fields to confirm a trust-auth approach will boot cleanly.

content.content:
1	from functools import lru_cache
2	from typing import Literal
3	
4	from pydantic import Field
5	from pydantic_settings import BaseSettings, SettingsConfigDict
6	
7	
8	class Settings(BaseSettings):
9	    model_config = SettingsConfigDict(
10	        # App config lives in .env.app (host dev reads it directly; compose injects
11	        # it into the api/worker containers via env_file). The root .env is
12	        # compose/infra-only (POSTGRES_*, VITE_*) and is NOT read here. extra=forbid
13	        # catches typo'd/stale keys — the split keeps those infra keys from tripping
14	        # it. See #209.
15	        env_file=".env.app",
16	        env_file_encoding="utf-8",
17	        extra="forbid",
18	        case_sensitive=False,
19	    )
20	
21	    environment: Literal["dev", "staging", "prod"] = "dev"
22	    log_level: Literal["DEBUG", "INFO", "WARNING", "ERROR", "CRITICAL"] = "INFO"
23	
24	    # No DB credentials embedded in code. The real URL (with password) comes from
25	    # the environment: docker-compose and CI set DATABASE_URL; local host dev gets
26	    # it from .env (setup.sh bootstraps + exports it). This default is only a
27	    # credential-less placeholder for the no-env case.
28	    database_url: str = Field(default="postgresql+psycopg2://localhost:5432/dataq")
29	    redis_url: str = Field(default="redis://localhost:6379/0")
30	
31	    applicationinsights_connection_string: str | None = None
32	
33	    sample_failures_retention_days: int = 30
34	
35	    # Stuck-run reaper (#309): a run committed `queued` (before `send_task`) — or
36	    # left `running` by a worker that died mid-execution — past this age is driven
37	    # to terminal `failed` by the beat janitor so it can't linger forever. Must
38	    # comfortably exceed the longest plausible suite run so a slow-but-alive run is
39	    # never reaped (a false reap self-corrects when the worker later commits its
40	    # real outcome, but would emit a spurious alert).
41	    stuck_run_threshold_minutes: int = 60
42	
43	    azure_tenant_id: str | None = None
44	    azure_api_client_id: str | None = None
45	    azure_spa_client_id: str | None = None
46	    azure_api_scope: str = "user_impersonation"
47	
48	    # Allow guest (B2B / external) identities in the tenant to authenticate.
49	    # Default off (fastapi-azure-auth's own secure default): the token validator
50	    # rejects guests with 403 "Guest users not allowed". Enable for deployments
51	    # whose legitimate users sign in with a guest account (e.g. a personal
52	    # Microsoft account invited into the tenant). Still bounded by tenant
53	    # membership + the API scope; orthogonal to WORKSPACE_ADMIN_EMAILS.
54	    azure_allow_guest_users: bool = False
55	
56	    auth_dev_bypass: bool = False
57	
58	    # Browser origins allowed to call the API cross-origin (the Static Web App ↔
59	    # Container Apps split in prod — PR #40 nit). Comma-separated; empty = no
60	    # cross-origin allowed (same-origin / dev proxy needs none). Provider-neutral:
61	    # it's a list of origins, not an Azure concept. Read via `cors_allow_origin_list`.
62	    #   CORS_ALLOW_ORIGINS=https://app.example.com,https://dataq.example.com
63	    cors_allow_origins: str = ""
64	
65	    # Public base URL of the deployed API (scheme+host, no trailing slash), used
66	    # to assemble the inbound orchestration webhook URLs the admin webhook-config
67	    # surface shows (#490). Set to the public host on deploy (e.g. the SWA origin
68	    # that proxies /api). Empty → fall back to the request's own base URL.
69	    #   PUBLIC_BASE_URL=https://dataq.example.com
70	    public_base_url: str = ""
71	
72	    # Workspace-admin allowlist — emails permitted to use the /admin read
73	    # endpoints (all-suites / all-users / access overview). Single-tenant, so this
74	    # is the whole-workspace admin set, distinct from the per-suite
75	    # view/edit/admin/owner ladder in suite_authz. Matched case-insensitively
76	    # against the IdP-supplied email — a generic identity attribute, so no
77	    # Azure/Entra claim is read in service code (ADR 0010/0013, CLAUDE.md §11).
78	    # Stored as a comma-separated string (not list[str]) to sidestep
79	    # pydantic-settings' JSON decoding of complex env values; read it via the
80	    # normalised `workspace_admin_email_set` property, never the raw field.
81	    #   WORKSPACE_ADMIN_EMAILS=ada@acme.io,grace@acme.io
82	    workspace_admin_emails: str = ""
83	
84	    secret_store: Literal["env", "redis", "azure_key_vault"] = (
85	        "env"  # noqa: S105 — mode selector, not a password
86	    )
87	    azure_key_vault_url: str | None = None
88	
89	    # SecretStore key holding the ADF webhook shared secret (ADR 0006). Resolved
90	    # via SecretStore.get → EnvSecretStore maps it to KV_SECRET_ADF_WEBHOOK_SECRET
91	    # in dev, Key Vault secret `adf-webhook-secret` in prod. Not the secret value.
92	    adf_webhook_secret_name: str = "adf-webhook-secret"  # noqa: S105 — KV key name, not a secret
93	    # SecretStore key holding the Airflow callback HMAC signing key (ADR 0007).
94	    # → KV_SECRET_AIRFLOW_WEBHOOK_SECRET in dev, KV secret `airflow-webhook-secret`
95	    # in prod. The signing key, not a webhook value.
96	    airflow_webhook_secret_name: str = "airflow-webhook-secret"  # noqa: S105 — KV key name
97	
98	    # SecretStore key holding the workspace MS Teams incoming-webhook URL (the URL
99	    # carries a token, so it lives in the SecretStore, not in config). Unset →
100	    # no Teams alerting (the no-op publisher). The value is the webhook URL,
101	    # resolved per run via SecretStore so a rotated webhook is picked up;
102	    # per-suite notification config (a later PR) extends the resolver. Provider-
103	    # neutral: Teams is one ResultPublisher impl behind the registry (ADR 0011).
104	    teams_webhook_secret_name: str | None = None
105	
106	    # SSRF allowlist for the per-suite Teams webhook URL. The webhook is supplied
107	    # by a suite editor and POSTed server-side, so its host is constrained to this
108	    # comma-separated set of suffixes (defaults to MS Teams incoming-webhook +
109	    # Power Automate workflow hosts; extend via env for a private relay). Stored as
110	    # a string — not list[str] — like workspace_admin_emails, to sidestep Pydantic

file.content:
from functools import lru_cache
from typing import Literal

from pydantic import Field
from pydantic_settings import BaseSettings, SettingsConfigDict


class Settings(BaseSettings):
    model_config = SettingsConfigDict(
        # App config lives in .env.app (host dev reads it directly; compose injects
        # it into the api/worker containers via env_file). The root .env is
        # compose/infra-only (POSTGRES_*, VITE_*) and is NOT read here. extra=forbid
        # catches typo'd/stale keys — the split keeps those infra keys from tripping
        # it. See #209.
        env_file=".env.app",
        env_file_encoding="utf-8",
        extra="forbid",
        case_sensitive=False,
    )

    environment: Literal["dev", "staging", "prod"] = "dev"
    log_level: Literal["DEBUG", "INFO", "WARNING", "ERROR", "CRITICAL"] = "INFO"

    # No DB credentials embedded in code. The real URL (with password) comes from
    # the environment: docker-compose and CI set DATABASE_URL; local host dev gets
    # it from .env (setup.sh bootstraps + exports it). This default is only a
    # credential-less placeholder for the no-env case.
    database_url: str = Field(default="postgresql+psycopg2://localhost:5432/dataq")
    redis_url: str = Field(default="redis://localhost:6379/0")

    applicationinsights_connection_string: str | None = None

    sample_failures_retention_days: int = 30

    # Stuck-run reaper (#309): a run committed `queued` (before `send_task`) — or
    # left `running` by a worker that died mid-execution — past this age is driven
    # to terminal `failed` by the beat janitor so it can't linger forever. Must
    # comfortably exceed the longest plausible suite run so a slow-but-alive run is
    # never reaped (a false reap self-corrects when the worker later commits its
    # real outcome, but would emit a spurious alert).
    stuck_run_threshold_minutes: int = 60

    azure_tenant_id: str | None = None
    azure_api_client_id: str | None = None
    azure_spa_client_id: str | None = None
    azure_api_scope: str = "user_impersonation"

    # Allow guest (B2B / external) identities in the tenant to authenticate.
    # Default off (fastapi-azure-auth's own secure default): the token validator
    # rejects guests with 403 "Guest users not allowed". Enable for deployments
    # whose legitimate users sign in with a guest account (e.g. a personal
    # Microsoft account invited into the tenant). Still bounded by tenant
    # membership + the API scope; orthogonal to WORKSPACE_ADMIN_EMAILS.
    azure_allow_guest_users: bool = False

    auth_dev_bypass: bool = False

    # Browser origins allowed to call the API cross-origin (the Static Web App ↔
    # Container Apps split in prod — PR #40 nit). Comma-separated; empty = no
    # cross-origin allowed (same-origin / dev proxy needs none). Provider-neutral:
    # it's a list of origins, not an Azure concept. Read via `cors_allow_origin_list`.
    #   CORS_ALLOW_ORIGINS=https://app.example.com,https://dataq.example.com
    cors_allow_origins: str = ""

    # Public base URL of the deployed API (scheme+host, no trailing slash), used
    # to assemble the inbound orchestration webhook URLs the admin webhook-config
    # surface shows (#490). Set to the public host on deploy (e.g. the SWA origin
    # that proxies /api). Empty → fall back to the request's own base URL.
    #   PUBLIC_BASE_URL=https://dataq.example.com
    public_base_url: str = ""

    # Workspace-admin allowlist — emails permitted to use the /admin read
    # endpoints (all-suites / all-users / access overview). Single-tenant, so this
    # is the whole-workspace admin set, distinct from the per-suite
    # view/edit/admin/owner ladder in suite_authz. Matched case-insensitively
    # against the IdP-supplied email — a generic identity attribute, so no
    # Azure/Entra claim is read in service code (ADR 0010/0013, CLAUDE.md §11).
    # Stored as a comma-separated string (not list[str]) to sidestep
    # pydantic-settings' JSON decoding of complex env values; read it via the
    # normalised `workspace_admin_email_set` property, never the raw field.
    #   WORKSPACE_ADMIN_EMAILS=ada@acme.io,grace@acme.io
    workspace_admin_emails: str = ""

    secret_store: Literal["env", "redis", "azure_key_vault"] = (
        "env"  # noqa: S105 — mode selector, not a password
    )
    azure_key_vault_url: str | None = None

    # SecretStore key holding the ADF webhook shared secret (ADR 0006). Resolved
    # via SecretStore.get → EnvSecretStore maps it to KV_SECRET_ADF_WEBHOOK_SECRET
    # in dev, Key Vault secret `adf-webhook-secret` in prod. Not the secret value.
    adf_webhook_secret_name: str = "adf-webhook-secret"  # noqa: S105 — KV key name, not a secret
    # SecretStore key holding the Airflow callback HMAC signing key (ADR 0007).
    # → KV_SECRET_AIRFLOW_WEBHOOK_SECRET in dev, KV secret `airflow-webhook-secret`
    # in prod. The signing key, not a webhook value.
    airflow_webhook_secret_name: str = "airflow-webhook-secret"  # noqa: S105 — KV key name

    # SecretStore key holding the workspace MS Teams incoming-webhook URL (the URL
    # carries a token, so it lives in the SecretStore, not in config). Unset →
    # no Teams alerting (the no-op publisher). The value is the webhook URL,
    # resolved per run via SecretStore so a rotated webhook is picked up;
    # per-suite notification config (a later PR) extends the resolver. Provider-
    # neutral: Teams is one ResultPublisher impl behind the registry (ADR 0011).
    teams_webhook_secret_name: str | None = None

    # SSRF allowlist for the per-suite Teams webhook URL. The webhook is supplied
    # by a suite editor and POSTed server-side, so its host is constrained to this
    # comma-separated set of suffixes (defaults to MS Teams incoming-webhook +
    # Power Automate workflow hosts; extend via env for a private relay). Stored as
    # a string — not list[str] — like workspace_admin_emails, to sidestep Pydantic

content.text: All settings have safe defaults — nothing required-without-default blocks startup. So a **trust-auth Postgres** (no embedded password) lets the no-clone stack boot while honoring the no-creds rule. Now writing the compose.

input.content:
# DataQ — run from prebuilt GHCR images, NO source checkout required (#472).
#
#   docker compose -f docker-compose.ghcr.yml up
#
# Brings up Postgres + Redis + the API + Celery worker + the UI, runs migrations
# and seeds demo data, all from images published to GHCR (ADR 0023). This is the
# "evaluate / self-host DataQ in 5 minutes" path; `git clone` + scripts/setup.sh
# remains the contributor path (docker-compose.yml, with source bind-mounts).
#
# Auth is dev-bypass (no Azure tenant needed) — every request resolves to a fixed
# dev user. The frontend image is the dev-bypass `:dev` build; the browser hits
# the UI and nginx reverse-proxies /api + /mcp to the api service (same-origin).
#
# Override the tags to pin a release instead of the moving stable pointers:
#   DATAQ_BACKEND_TAG=v1.0.0 DATAQ_FRONTEND_TAG=v1.0.0 docker compose -f ... up
#
# NOT for production: Postgres runs trust-auth (no password — so no credential
# ships in this tracked file) and Redis is open on the compose network. A real
# deploy uses the Terraform stack (deploy/terraform, ADR 0024).

x-backend-image: &backend-image "ghcr.io/theurgicduke771/dataq-backend:${DATAQ_BACKEND_TAG:-latest}"

# Shared app env for api/worker/migrate. No .env files are read on this path, so
# every key is inline. dataq@postgres has no password (trust auth); user + db
# names are non-secret identifiers. SECRET_STORE=redis (NOT env): api and worker
# are separate containers, and EnvSecretStore is per-process — a credential the
# api writes at runtime would be invisible to the worker (#86). Redis shares it.
x-app-env: &app-env
  DATABASE_URL: "postgresql+psycopg2://dataq@postgres:5432/dataq"
  REDIS_URL: "redis://redis:6379/0"
  SECRET_STORE: "redis"
  AUTH_DEV_BYPASS: "true"

services:
  postgres:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    restart: unless-stopped
    environment:
      # trust-auth: no password set or required (eval only). Keeps any credential
      # out of this git-tracked file (CLAUDE.md). user/db are non-secret names.
      POSTGRES_HOST_AUTH_METHOD: trust
      POSTGRES_USER: dataq
      POSTGRES_DB: dataq
    ports:
      - "5432:5432"
    volumes:
      - postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U dataq"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 3s
      retries: 10

  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    restart: unless-stopped
    ports:
      - "6379:6379"
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "redis-cli", "ping"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 3s
      retries: 10

  # One-shot: migrate to head THEN seed the dev user + probe fixtures + demo data
  # (seed_dev is idempotent, safe to re-run on every `up`). api/worker gate on its
  # successful completion so the stack never serves an un-migrated/empty schema.
  # Gets the full app env because the seed writes connection secrets via the
  # (redis-backed) SecretStore.
  migrate:
    image: *backend-image
    restart: "no"
    command: sh -c "cd backend && alembic upgrade head && cd .. && python -m backend.scripts.seed_dev"
    environment: *app-env
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy
      redis:
        condition: service_healthy

  api:
    image: *backend-image
    restart: unless-stopped
    command: uvicorn backend.app.main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000
    ports:
      - "8000:8000"
    environment: *app-env
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy
      redis:
        condition: service_healthy
      migrate:
        condition: service_completed_successfully

  worker:
    image: *backend-image
    restart: unless-stopped
    # -B runs celery-beat embedded (orchestration polling, scheduled dispatch).
    # Fine for the single-worker eval stack; prod runs a separate beat.
    command: celery -A backend.app.worker.celery_app worker -B --loglevel=INFO
    environment: *app-env
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy
      redis:
        condition: service_healthy
      migrate:
        condition: service_completed_successfully

  # The dev-bypass UI image (nginx). Serves the SPA and reverse-proxies /api +
  # /mcp to the api service (DATAQ_API_UPSTREAM defaults to http://api:8000, baked
  # into the image). Browse the whole app same-origin at http://localhost:3000.
  frontend:
    image: "ghcr.io/theurgicduke771/dataq-frontend:${DATAQ_FRONTEND_TAG:-dev}"
    restart: unless-stopped
    ports:
      - "3000:8080"
    depends_on:
      - api

volumes:
  postgres_data:

file.content:
publish:
    name: Build + push ${{ matrix.name }} (amd64 + arm64)
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      matrix:
        include:
          # Backend build context is the repo ROOT (the app imports as the
          # top-level `backend.app.*` package — see backend/Dockerfile).
          - name: backend
            dockerfile: backend/Dockerfile
            context: .
          # Frontend build context is frontend/ (its Dockerfile COPYs from there).
          - name: frontend
            dockerfile: frontend/Dockerfile
            context: frontend
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v7

      # QEMU provides the arm64 emulation the amd64 runner needs to build the
      # non-native platform; buildx drives the multi-platform build + manifest.
      - uses: docker/setup-qemu-action@v3
      - uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v3

      - name: Log in to GHCR
        uses: docker/login-action@v4
        with:
          registry: ghcr.io
          username: ${{ github.actor }}
          password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

      - name: Compute image tags
        id: tags
        # Untrusted inputs come in via env (never interpolated into the script
        # body) so a dispatch `tag` value can't inject shell — IMAGE_NAME is the
        # static matrix name, INPUT_TAG the user-supplied one.
        env:
          IMAGE_NAME: ${{ matrix.name }}
          INPUT_TAG: ${{ inputs.tag }}
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          image="${REGISTRY}/${OWNER}/dataq-${IMAGE_NAME}"
          # Always: the moving stable pointer + the immutable commit SHA.
          tags="${image}:latest"$'\n'"${image}:${GITHUB_SHA}"
          # On a release-tag push, github.ref_name is the tag (vX.Y.Z); on a
          # manual run, the optional `tag` input. Publish it as a third tag.
          extra="${INPUT_TAG}"
          if [ -z "$extra" ] && [ "${GITHUB_REF_TYPE}" = "tag" ]; then
            extra="${GITHUB_REF_NAME}"
          fi
          # Validate the extra tag against Docker's tag grammar (alphanumerics +
          # _.- , ≤128 chars, no leading separator) before it reaches the image ref.
          if [ -n "$extra" ] && ! printf '%s' "$extra" | grep -Eq '^[A-Za-z0-9_][A-Za-z0-9_.-]{0,127}$'; then
            echo "::error::Invalid image tag '${extra}' — must match Docker's tag grammar."
            exit 1
          fi
          if [ -n "$extra" ]; then
            tags="${tags}"$'\n'"${image}:${extra}"
          fi
          echo "Publishing tags:"; echo "$tags"
          # Multi-line output for the build-push-action `tags:` list.
          {
            echo "tags<<EOF"
            echo "$tags"
            echo "EOF"
          } >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

      - name: Build + push (multi-arch)
        uses: docker/build-push-action@v6
        with:
          context: ${{ matrix.context }}
          file: ${{ matrix.dockerfile }}
          platforms: linux/amd64,linux/arm64
          push: true
          tags: ${{ steps.tags.outputs.tags }}
          # Attestations off: keep the published manifest a plain multi-arch index
          # (no extra unknown/attestation entries) so anonymous ACA/compose pulls
          # resolve cleanly.
          provenance: false

input.new_string:
publish:
    name: Build + push ${{ matrix.name }} ${{ matrix.variant }} (amd64 + arm64)
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      matrix:
        include:
          # Backend build context is the repo ROOT (the app imports as the
          # top-level `backend.app.*` package — see backend/Dockerfile). Its auth
          # mode is a runtime env var, so one image serves every deployment.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
name: Publish images (GHCR, multi-arch)

# Builds + publishes the BYOL distribution images to GHCR — multi-arch
# (linux/amd64 + linux/arm64) so Apple-Silicon devs pull a native image, not an
# emulated one (#472). ADR 0023 (GHCR is the BYOL registry) / ADR 0013.
#
# This is DELIBERATELY separate from deploy.yml: that workflow builds an
# amd64-only, SHA-tagged BACKEND image for the Azure rollout (ACA is amd64, the
# immutable SHA tag is correct there). This one publishes BOTH images, multi-arch,
# under a moving `:latest` stable pointer for `docker compose ... up`-from-image
# users. The two never both fire on the same event (deploy is manual-dispatch).
#
# Stable tag to pull = `:latest` (moving). The immutable per-deploy `:vN` backend
# tags (v7, v10, …) stay owned by deploy.yml — this workflow never writes them.
# A release-tag push (vX.Y.Z) additionally publishes that semver tag.

on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      tag:
        description: "Extra immutable tag to publish alongside :latest + the commit SHA (e.g. v1.0.0). Blank → just :latest + SHA."
        default: ""
  push:
    tags:
      - "v*"

concurrency:
  group: publish-images
  cancel-in-progress: false

# GITHUB_TOKEN needs packages:write to push to the repo-linked GHCR packages
# (ADR 0023). No Azure/registry credential is involved.
permissions:
  contents: read
  packages: write

env:
  REGISTRY: ghcr.io
  # Lowercase owner — GHCR paths are case-sensitive and must be lowercase.
  OWNER: theurgicduke771

jobs:
  publish:
    name: Build + push ${{ matrix.name }} (amd64 + arm64)
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      matrix:
        include:
          # Backend build context is the repo ROOT (the app imports as the
          # top-level `backend.app.*` package — see backend/Dockerfile).
          - name: backend
            dockerfile: backend/Dockerfile
            context: .
          # Frontend build context is frontend/ (its Dockerfile COPYs from there).
          - name: frontend
            dockerfile: frontend/Dockerfile
            context: frontend
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v7

      # QEMU provides the arm64 emulation the amd64 runner needs to build the
      # non-native platform; buildx drives the multi-platform build + manifest.
      - uses: docker/setup-qemu-action@v3
      - uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v3

      - name: Log in to GHCR
        uses: docker/login-action@v4
        with:
          registry: ghcr.io
          username: ${{ github.actor }}
          password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

      - name: Compute image tags
        id: tags
        # Untrusted inputs come in via env (never interpolated into the script
        # body) so a dispatch `tag` value can't inject shell — IMAGE_NAME is the
        # static matrix name, INPUT_TAG the user-supplied one.
        env:
          IMAGE_NAME: ${{ matrix.name }}
          INPUT_TAG: ${{ inputs.tag }}
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          image="${REGISTRY}/${OWNER}/dataq-${IMAGE_NAME}"
          # Always: the moving stable pointer + the immutable commit SHA.
          tags="${image}:latest"$'\n'"${image}:${GITHUB_SHA}"
          # On a release-tag push, github.ref_name is the tag (vX.Y.Z); on a
          # manual run, the optional `tag` input. Publish it as a third tag.
          extra="${INPUT_TAG}"
          if [ -z "$extra" ] && [ "${GITHUB_REF_TYPE}" = "tag" ]; then
            extra="${GITHUB_REF_NAME}"
          fi
          # Validate the extra tag against Docker's tag grammar (alphanumerics +
          # _.- , ≤128 chars, no leading separator) before it reaches the image ref.
          if [ -n "$extra" ] && ! printf '%s' "$extra" | grep -Eq '^[A-Za-z0-9_][A-Za-z0-9_.-]{0,127}$'; then
            echo "::error::Invalid image tag '${extra}' — must match Docker's tag grammar."
            exit 1
          fi
          if [ -n "$extra" ]; then
            tags="${tags}"$'\n'"${image}:${extra}"
          fi
          echo "Publishing tags:"; echo "$tags"
          # Multi-line output for the build-push-action `tags:` list.
          {
            echo "tags<<EOF"
            echo "$tags"
            echo "EOF"
          } >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

      - name: Build + push (multi-arch)
        uses: docker/build-push-action@v6
        with:
          context: ${{ matrix.context }}
          file: ${{ matrix.dockerfile }}
          platforms: linux/amd64,linux/arm64
          push: true
          tags: ${{ steps.tags.outputs.tags }}
          # Attestations off: keep the published manifest a plain multi-arch index
          # (no extra unknown/attestation entries) so anonymous ACA/compose pulls
          # resolve cleanly.
          provenance: false

lines: +    name: Build + push ${{ matrix.name }} ${{ matrix.variant }} (amd64 + arm64)
# Backend build context is the repo ROOT (the app imports as the
+          # top-level `backend.app.*` package — see backend/Dockerfile). Its auth
+          # mode is a runtime env var, so one image serves every deployment.
# Frontend build context is frontend/ (its Dockerfile COPYs from there).
+          # The SPA bakes auth mode at build time, so it ships as TWO images:
+          #   standard → :latest (+ sha / release tag): production build, no
+          #              bypass. Real-auth self-host rebuilds with their VITE_AZURE_*.
+          #   dev → :dev only: NODE_ENV=development (→ import.meta.env.DEV true, so
+          #         the dev-bypass auth gate activates) + VITE_AUTH_DEV_BYPASS=true.
+          #         The zero-config eval image docker-compose.ghcr.yml pulls.

content.text: Let me validate the workflow YAML, then build the `:dev` frontend locally (tagged as the GHCR ref) so I can test the full compose against the real backend `:latest`.

input.command:
cd /Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ
echo "=== YAML parse: workflow + compose ==="
conda run -n dataq python -c "import yaml; yaml.safe_load(open('.github/workflows/publish-images.yml')); yaml.safe_load(open('docker-compose.ghcr.yml')); print('both YAML OK')"
echo "=== docker compose config validate ==="
docker compose -f docker-compose.ghcr.yml config >/dev/null 2>&1 && echo "compose config valid" || docker compose -f docker-compose.ghcr.yml config 2>&1 | tail -5
echo "=== build :dev frontend, tag as GHCR ref ==="
docker build -q -f frontend/Dockerfile --build-arg BUILD_NODE_ENV=development --build-arg VITE_AUTH_DEV_BYPASS=true -t ghcr.io/theurgicduke771/dataq-frontend:dev frontend >/dev/null 2>&1 && echo "frontend:dev built+tagged"
input.description: Validate YAML/compose config and build :dev frontend

content.text: Found it: `fe_sendauth: no password supplied` — the `postgres_data` volume name **collides with the dev compose's volume** (same project dir → same `dataq_postgres_data`). That volume was initialized by the dev stack *with* a password, and Postgres auth config is fixed at init-time, so trust never took effect. Let me give the eval stack its own volume and tear down the dirty state.

input.old_string:
volumes:
      - postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U dataq"]
input.new_string:
volumes:
      # Distinct from docker-compose.yml's volume: this stack runs trust-auth, so
      # it must never reuse a password-initialized data dir (auth config is fixed
      # at init time) — sharing the name would collide in the same project dir.
      - ghcr_postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U dataq"]
