content.content:
1	# DataQ — deployment guide
2	
3	How DataQ v1 is deployed to Azure. Infrastructure is **in-repo Terraform**
4	(`deploy/terraform/`, applied — [ADR 0024](../docs/adr/0024-app-deployment-infrastructure.md));
5	the app rolls out via the **`Deploy`** workflow
6	([.github/workflows/deploy.yml](../.github/workflows/deploy.yml), `workflow_dispatch`).
7	The stack is **live** — this is the runbook to provision a fresh environment and to
8	deploy a new image. Related: [ADR 0025](../docs/adr/0025-production-image-pip-slim.md)
9	(slim+pip image), [ADR 0023](../docs/adr/0023-container-image-registry-ghcr.md) (GHCR).
10	
11	Azure is **one** deploy target behind the app's seams (ADR 0010/0013) — the
12	manifests here are infra config, not business logic. No Azure resource names are
13	hardcoded in app code; they live only as Terraform vars + workflow `vars`/`secrets`.
14	
15	## Topology
16	
17	```
18	Browser ─► Azure Static Web App (frontend/dist)
19	              │  /api/* proxied to the linked backend (same-origin → no CORS)
20	              ▼
21	        Azure Container Apps
22	          • dataq-api     (FastAPI image, external ingress :8000)
23	          • dataq-worker  (same image, `celery -A ... worker` + beat)
24	          • dataq-migrate (Container Apps Job: `alembic upgrade head`)
25	              │
26	              ├─► Azure Database for PostgreSQL (DATABASE_URL)
27	              ├─► Azure Cache for Redis        (REDIS_URL)
28	              ├─► Azure Key Vault              (SECRET_STORE=azure_key_vault, managed identity)
29	              └─► Application Insights         (APPLICATIONINSIGHTS_CONNECTION_STRING)
30	        GitHub Container Registry (GHCR) — holds the backend image (ADR 0023)
31	          ghcr.io/theurgicduke771/dataq-backend:<tag> — public package, so ACA
32	          pulls it anonymously (no registry credential stored on the apps/job).
33	```
34	
35	Both api + worker run the **same** image ([backend/Dockerfile](../backend/Dockerfile),
36	build context = repo root). The frontend is built and uploaded to SWA by the
37	workflow (the [frontend/Dockerfile](../frontend/Dockerfile) is the container
38	alternative — only needed if you run the UI on Container Apps instead of SWA).
39	
40	## One-time provisioning
41	
42	The datasource + compute infra is stood up by the external Terraform harness
43	(ADR 0021) — see `HARNESS_TODO.md`. Beyond that, this app needs:
44	
45	1. An **ACA environment** + the three apps/job above (the backend image is on
46	   **GHCR**, not ACR — ADR 0023). The api/worker run `uvicorn …` / `celery …`;
47	   the migrate **job** runs `alembic upgrade head`. The `deploy/terraform/` stack
48	   provisions all of this; the GHCR package must be **public** so ACA pulls it
49	   anonymously.
50	2. **Managed identity** on the api + worker apps with **Key Vault Secrets User**
51	   on the vault (so `DefaultAzureCredential` resolves `SECRET_STORE=azure_key_vault`).
52	3. **App env**: set the keys on the api + worker apps. The **complete** env-var
53	   reference (every Settings key) is [../.env.app.example](../.env.app.example);
54	   the prod-specific *values* are in [deploy/.env.app.prod.example](.env.app.prod.example).
55	   Secret values (DB/Redis URL, App Insights, webhook URLs) are Key Vault-backed
56	   Container Apps secrets — never literals. The user-assigned managed identity
57	   needs `AZURE_CLIENT_ID` set so `DefaultAzureCredential` resolves it (#408).
58	4. **SWA linked backend**: link the `dataq-api` Container App as the SWA backend so
59	   `/api/*` is proxied same-origin (then `CORS_ALLOW_ORIGINS` can stay empty). If
60	   instead the SPA calls the API cross-origin, set `CORS_ALLOW_ORIGINS` to the SWA
61	   origin (the FastAPI CORS middleware turns on only when it's non-empty).
62	5. **Azure Monitor → ADF webhook** alert rule (Week-7 task) — needs the deployed
63	   API URL; configure after the first deploy. Per [ADR 0006](../docs/adr/0006-adf-webhook-authentication.md)
64	   the shared secret rides the URL as a `?token=` query param, so don't
65	   hand-assemble it (wrong host / stale token after rotation / missing `?token=`
66	   are easy to get wrong — #92).
67	
68	   **Easiest path: the in-app webhook-config surface (#490).** Sign in as a
69	   workspace admin → **Settings → Webhooks** to copy the ready-to-paste ADF
70	   URL (host + current `?token=` from Key Vault) and the Airflow URL. Set
71	   `PUBLIC_BASE_URL` so the generated host is the public origin (the deploy sets
72	   it to the SWA host; empty falls back to the request host). Paste the ADF URL
73	   into the Action Group webhook field. Note the **live ADF delivery still needs
74	   the Common-Alert-Schema payload mapping (#492)** — the alert body Azure Monitor
75	   sends differs from what the receiver parses today.
76	
77	   Or build it from the CLI (the live host + Key Vault secret):
78	
79	   ```bash
80	   # Vars you already set for the deploy workflow + the vault name.
81	   RG=<AZURE_RESOURCE_GROUP>; API_APP_NAME=<API_APP_NAME>; VAULT=<key-vault-name>
82	   API_HOST=$(az containerapp show -n "$API_APP_NAME" -g "$RG" \
83	     --query properties.configuration.ingress.fqdn -o tsv)
84	   # ADF_WEBHOOK_SECRET_NAME (default 'adf-webhook-secret') is the Key Vault *key*.
85	   TOKEN=$(az keyvault secret show --vault-name "$VAULT" --name adf-webhook-secret \
86	     --query value -o tsv)
87	   printf 'ADF webhook URL: https://%s/api/v1/orchestration/events/adf?token=%s\n' \
88	     "$API_HOST" "$TOKEN"
89	   ```
90	
91	   ⚠️ The printed URL **contains the shared secret**. Paste it straight into the
92	   Action Group webhook config; never commit it, and don't run this where the
93	   output is captured to a log (CI, `script`, screen-share). The secret has a
94	   single source of truth (Key Vault), so re-run after a rotation
95	   ([ADR 0006](../docs/adr/0006-adf-webhook-authentication.md) is a hard cutover).
96	
97	   The token is placed in the URL **un-encoded**, and the receiver compares the
98	   *URL-decoded* `token` against the Key Vault value — so the webhook secret must
99	   be **URL-safe** (generate it as e.g. `openssl rand -hex 32`). If an existing
100	   secret contains reserved characters (`+` `/` `=` `&` `#` space), percent-encode
101	   the token in the pasted URL, or it will silently fail auth (401).
102	
103	   The **Airflow** callback URL is the sibling endpoint but carries **no secret**
104	   — it's HMAC-signed in a header ([ADR 0007](../docs/adr/0007-airflow-callback-model.md)),
105	   with the signing key configured in the DAG snippet ([integrations/airflow/](../integrations/airflow/)),
106	   not the URL — so it's just `https://$API_HOST/api/v1/orchestration/events/airflow`.
107	
108	## GitHub config the workflow reads
109	
110	Set under repo **Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions**, and add a
111	federated credential for OIDC login (subject = this repo's `production`
112	environment).
113	
114	**Secrets:** `AZURE_CLIENT_ID`, `AZURE_TENANT_ID`, `AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID`
115	(OIDC app registration), `AZURE_STATIC_WEB_APPS_API_TOKEN`. The GHCR image push
116	uses the built-in `GITHUB_TOKEN` (`packages: write`) — no registry secret to set.
117	
118	**Variables:** `AZURE_RESOURCE_GROUP`, `API_APP_NAME`, `WORKER_APP_NAME`,
119	`MIGRATE_JOB_NAME`, and the non-secret `VITE_AZURE_*` build values. (No `ACR_*` —
120	the image lives on GHCR at a fixed `ghcr.io/theurgicduke771/dataq-backend` path.)
121	
122	## Going live
123	
124	1. Provision the resources + set the secrets/vars above.
125	2. Run the **Deploy** workflow manually (`workflow_dispatch`) to validate end-to-end.
126	3. To deploy on every merge, uncomment the `push: branches: [main]` trigger in
127	   the workflow.
128	
129	Migrations are additive/backward-compatible (CLAUDE.md), so the workflow runs
130	`alembic upgrade head` **before** rolling the apps — the running old code
131	tolerates the new schema. (This is exactly the dev-DB step that, when skipped,
132	500s the checks endpoint after a schema-adding deploy.)
133	
134	Use an **immutable** `image_tag` per release — ACA caches a tag at the node, so a
135	same-tag rebuild won't be re-pulled on a new revision. Push-on-merge is
136	intentionally **off**; deploys are manual `workflow_dispatch`.
137	
138	## Verify
139	
140	- `GET /healthz` → `200 {"status":"ok"}`.
141	- `GET /api/v1/me` → **401** (auth enforced; a valid token resolves the user).
142	- SPA root + a deep link → 200; sign-in via Azure AD SSO.
143	- Interactive API docs (`/docs`, `/redoc`, `/openapi.json`) are **404 in prod**
144	  (#170 — the prod-docs gate); the MCP server is mounted at `/mcp` (Azure-AD
145	  validated; fail-closed without auth — [ADR 0008](../docs/adr/0008-mcp-server.md)).
146	- Celery beat starts clean (no `NoneType` lock crash, #405/#407) and orchestration
147	  polling reads Key Vault secrets (#406/#408).
148	
149	## Operational notes
150	
151	- **Restart dependent Container Apps after a shared-Postgres delete/recreate** —
152	  the DB host is injected as a start-time secret snapshot, so every dependent
153	  revision must be restarted or it keeps resolving the old/dead host.
154	- The shared RG / Container Apps env / Postgres server are **reused, never
155	  destroyed** (free/trial caps one of each; shared with the harness — ADR 0024).
156	- `key_vault_purge_protection` is off during bring-up (so a destroy/re-apply can
157	  reuse the vault name); set it **true** for a hardened prod (irreversible).
158	- **Workspace-admins are superusers over every suite** ([ADR 0027](../docs/adr/0027-suite-permission-model-workspace-admin.md) / #482):
159	  anyone in `WORKSPACE_ADMIN_EMAILS` can read **all** suites' results — including
160	  failing-row samples (`results.sample_failures`), the one place PII/PHI lands —
161	  and manage/delete any suite. **Keep the allowlist minimal.** For a **PHI / regulated
162	  deployment**, treat the data-access audit trail (G1 / #431 in
163	  [compliance-posture.md](../docs/compliance-posture.md)) as a **prerequisite before
164	  granting broad workspace-admin** — PHI is already G1-blocked, and this access breadth
165	  raises the bar.
166

file.content:
# DataQ — deployment guide

How DataQ v1 is deployed to Azure. Infrastructure is **in-repo Terraform**
(`deploy/terraform/`, applied — [ADR 0024](../docs/adr/0024-app-deployment-infrastructure.md));
the app rolls out via the **`Deploy`** workflow
([.github/workflows/deploy.yml](../.github/workflows/deploy.yml), `workflow_dispatch`).
The stack is **live** — this is the runbook to provision a fresh environment and to
deploy a new image. Related: [ADR 0025](../docs/adr/0025-production-image-pip-slim.md)
(slim+pip image), [ADR 0023](../docs/adr/0023-container-image-registry-ghcr.md) (GHCR).

Azure is **one** deploy target behind the app's seams (ADR 0010/0013) — the
manifests here are infra config, not business logic. No Azure resource names are
hardcoded in app code; they live only as Terraform vars + workflow `vars`/`secrets`.

## Topology

```
Browser ─► Azure Static Web App (frontend/dist)
              │  /api/* proxied to the linked backend (same-origin → no CORS)
              ▼
        Azure Container Apps
          • dataq-api     (FastAPI image, external ingress :8000)
          • dataq-worker  (same image, `celery -A ... worker` + beat)
          • dataq-migrate (Container Apps Job: `alembic upgrade head`)
              │
              ├─► Azure Database for PostgreSQL (DATABASE_URL)
              ├─► Azure Cache for Redis        (REDIS_URL)
              ├─► Azure Key Vault              (SECRET_STORE=azure_key_vault, managed identity)
              └─► Application Insights         (APPLICATIONINSIGHTS_CONNECTION_STRING)
        GitHub Container Registry (GHCR) — holds the backend image (ADR 0023)
          ghcr.io/theurgicduke771/dataq-backend:<tag> — public package, so ACA
          pulls it anonymously (no registry credential stored on the apps/job).
```

Both api + worker run the **same** image ([backend/Dockerfile](../backend/Dockerfile),
build context = repo root). The frontend is built and uploaded to SWA by the
workflow (the [frontend/Dockerfile](../frontend/Dockerfile) is the container
alternative — only needed if you run the UI on Container Apps instead of SWA).

## One-time provisioning

The datasource + compute infra is stood up by the external Terraform harness
(ADR 0021) — see `HARNESS_TODO.md`. Beyond that, this app needs:

1. An **ACA environment** + the three apps/job above (the backend image is on
   **GHCR**, not ACR — ADR 0023). The api/worker run `uvicorn …` / `celery …`;
   the migrate **job** runs `alembic upgrade head`. The `deploy/terraform/` stack
   provisions all of this; the GHCR package must be **public** so ACA pulls it
   anonymously.
2. **Managed identity** on the api + worker apps with **Key Vault Secrets User**
   on the vault (so `DefaultAzureCredential` resolves `SECRET_STORE=azure_key_vault`).
3. **App env**: set the keys on the api + worker apps. The **complete** env-var
   reference (every Settings key) is [../.env.app.example](../.env.app.example);
   the prod-specific *values* are in [deploy/.env.app.prod.example](.env.app.prod.example).
   Secret values (DB/Redis URL, App Insights, webhook URLs) are Key Vault-backed
   Container Apps secrets — never literals. The user-assigned managed identity
   needs `AZURE_CLIENT_ID` set so `DefaultAzureCredential` resolves it (#408).
4. **SWA linked backend**: link the `dataq-api` Container App as the SWA backend so
   `/api/*` is proxied same-origin (then `CORS_ALLOW_ORIGINS` can stay empty). If
   instead the SPA calls the API cross-origin, set `CORS_ALLOW_ORIGINS` to the SWA
   origin (the FastAPI CORS middleware turns on only when it's non-empty).
5. **Azure Monitor → ADF webhook** alert rule (Week-7 task) — needs the deployed
   API URL; configure after the first deploy. Per [ADR 0006](../docs/adr/0006-adf-webhook-authentication.md)
   the shared secret rides the URL as a `?token=` query param, so don't
   hand-assemble it (wrong host / stale token after rotation / missing `?token=`
   are easy to get wrong — #92).

   **Easiest path: the in-app webhook-config surface (#490).** Sign in as a
   workspace admin → **Settings → Webhooks** to copy the ready-to-paste ADF
   URL (host + current `?token=` from Key Vault) and the Airflow URL. Set
   `PUBLIC_BASE_URL` so the generated host is the public origin (the deploy sets
   it to the SWA host; empty falls back to the request host). Paste the ADF URL
   into the Action Group webhook field. Note the **live ADF delivery still needs
   the Common-Alert-Schema payload mapping (#492)** — the alert body Azure Monitor
   sends differs from what the receiver parses today.

   Or build it from the CLI (the live host + Key Vault secret):

   ```bash
   # Vars you already set for the deploy workflow + the vault name.
   RG=<AZURE_RESOURCE_GROUP>; API_APP_NAME=<API_APP_NAME>; VAULT=<key-vault-name>
   API_HOST=$(az containerapp show -n "$API_APP_NAME" -g "$RG" \
     --query properties.configuration.ingress.fqdn -o tsv)
   # ADF_WEBHOOK_SECRET_NAME (default 'adf-webhook-secret') is the Key Vault *key*.
   TOKEN=$(az keyvault secret show --vault-name "$VAULT" --name adf-webhook-secret \
     --query value -o tsv)
   printf 'ADF webhook URL: https://%s/api/v1/orchestration/events/adf?token=%s\n' \
     "$API_HOST" "$TOKEN"
   ```

   ⚠️ The printed URL **contains the shared secret**. Paste it straight into the
   Action Group webhook config; never commit it, and don't run this where the
   output is captured to a log (CI, `script`, screen-share). The secret has a
   single source of truth (Key Vault), so re-run after a rotation
   ([ADR 0006](../docs/adr/0006-adf-webhook-authentication.md) is a hard cutover).

   The token is placed in the URL **un-encoded**, and the receiver compares the
   *URL-decoded* `token` against the Key Vault value — so the webhook secret must
   be **URL-safe** (generate it as e.g. `openssl rand -hex 32`). If an existing
   secret contains reserved characters (`+` `/` `=` `&` `#` space), percent-encode
   the token in the pasted URL, or it will silently fail auth (401).

   The **Airflow** callback URL is the sibling endpoint but carries **no secret**
   — it's HMAC-signed in a header ([ADR 0007](../docs/adr/0007-airflow-callback-model.md)),
   with the signing key configured in the DAG snippet ([integrations/airflow/](../integrations/airflow/)),
   not the URL — so it's just `https://$API_HOST/api/v1/orchestration/events/airflow`.

## GitHub config the workflow reads

Set under repo **Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions**, and add a
federated credential for OIDC login (subject = this repo's `production`
environment).

**Secrets:** `AZURE_CLIENT_ID`, `AZURE_TENANT_ID`, `AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID`
(OIDC app registration), `AZURE_STATIC_WEB_APPS_API_TOKEN`. The GHCR image push
uses the built-in `GITHUB_TOKEN` (`packages: write`) — no registry secret to set.

**Variables:** `AZURE_RESOURCE_GROUP`, `API_APP_NAME`, `WORKER_APP_NAME`,
`MIGRATE_JOB_NAME`, and the non-secret `VITE_AZURE_*` build values. (No `ACR_*` —
the image lives on GHCR at a fixed `ghcr.io/theurgicduke771/dataq-backend` path.)

## Going live

1. Provision the resources + set the secrets/vars above.
2. Run the **Deploy** workflow manually (`workflow_dispatch`) to validate end-to-end.
3. To deploy on every merge, uncomment the `push: branches: [main]` trigger in
   the workflow.

Migrations are additive/backward-compatible (CLAUDE.md), so the workflow runs
`alembic upgrade head` **before** rolling the apps — the running old code
tolerates the new schema. (This is exactly the dev-DB step that, when skipped,
500s the checks endpoint after a schema-adding deploy.)

Use an **immutable** `image_tag` per release — ACA caches a tag at the node, so a
same-tag rebuild won't be re-pulled on a new revision. Push-on-merge is
intentionally **off**; deploys are manual `workflow_dispatch`.

## Verify

- `GET /healthz` → `200 {"status":"ok"}`.
- `GET /api/v1/me` → **401** (auth enforced; a valid token resolves the user).
- SPA root + a deep link → 200; sign-in via Azure AD SSO.
- Interactive API docs (`/docs`, `/redoc`, `/openapi.json`) are **404 in prod**
  (#170 — the prod-docs gate); the MCP server is mounted at `/mcp` (Azure-AD
  validated; fail-closed without auth — [ADR 0008](../docs/adr/0008-mcp-server.md)).
- Celery beat starts clean (no `NoneType` lock crash, #405/#407) and orchestration
  polling reads Key Vault secrets (#406/#408).

## Operational notes

- **Restart dependent Container Apps after a shared-Postgres delete/recreate** —
  the DB host is injected as a start-time secret snapshot, so every dependent
  revision must be restarted or it keeps resolving the old/dead host.
- The shared RG / Container Apps env / Postgres server are **reused, never
  destroyed** (free/trial caps one of each; shared with the harness — ADR 0024).
- `key_vault_purge_protection` is off during bring-up (so a destroy/re-apply can
  reuse the vault name); set it **true** for a hardened prod (irreversible).
- **Workspace-admins are superusers over every suite** ([ADR 0027](../docs/adr/0027-suite-permission-model-workspace-admin.md) / #482):
  anyone in `WORKSPACE_ADMIN_EMAILS` can read **all** suites' results — including
  failing-row samples (`results.sample_failures`), the one place PII/PHI lands —
  and manage/delete any suite. **Keep the allowlist minimal.** For a **PHI / regulated
  deployment**, treat the data-access audit trail (G1 / #431 in
  [compliance-posture.md](../docs/compliance-posture.md)) as a **prerequisite before
  granting broad workspace-admin** — PHI is already G1-blocked, and this access breadth
  raises the bar.

content.text:
tfstate is **not** tracked (only `terraform.tfvars.example`) — no leak there. I now have what I need: the prod env keys and Terraform vars. Here's my plan for your production-docs request.

**Home:** `deploy/README.md` (the existing deploy runbook, which the docs site already links to) — I'll add a **"Before you deploy: production prerequisites"** section up top, as its own PR (separate from #472).

**Structure I'll write:**

1. **What you must change before production** (grounded in [deploy/.env.app.prod.example](deploy/.env.app.prod.example)) — the critical toggles: `AUTH_DEV_BYPASS=false` (never ship bypass), real Azure AD SSO (`AZURE_TENANT_ID` / `AZURE_API_CLIENT_ID` / `AZURE_SPA_CLIENT_ID`), **rebuild the frontend with your `VITE_AZURE_*`** (the prebuilt `:dev`/`:latest` images are eval-only — ties back to #472), `SECRET_STORE=azure_key_vault` + `AZURE_KEY_VAULT_URL` + the managed-identity `AZURE_CLIENT_ID`, Key-Vault-backed `DATABASE_URL`/`REDIS_URL`, `CORS_ALLOW_ORIGINS`/SWA linking, `PUBLIC_BASE_URL`, minimal `WORKSPACE_ADMIN_EMAILS`, and hardening toggles (`key_vault_purge_protection=true`, prod-docs 404 gate).

2. **Access required** — Azure subscription rights (create RG/ACA/Postgres/Redis/Key Vault/App Insights/SWA + register resource providers), Azure AD rights (create 2 app registrations + grant admin consent), a Key Vault **Secrets User** role assignment for the managed identity, and GitHub rights (repo admin for Actions secrets/vars + the OIDC federated credential; `packages:write` for GHCR).

3. **Cloud prerequisites**, per your ask:
   - **Azure** — supported today (detailed: subscription, region quota for 1 ACA env + 1 Flexible Postgres + KV + App Insights + SWA, RP registration, OIDC app-reg, public GHCR package).
   - **AWS — planned / not yet available** — noted with the likely seam-mapped services (ECS-Fargate/App Runner + RDS + ElastiCache + Secrets Manager + CloudWatch), per the anti-lock-in seams (ADR 0010/0013).
   - **GCP — planned / not yet available** — likewise (Cloud Run + Cloud SQL + Memorystore + Secret Manager + Cloud Logging).

I'll write it right after PR3 merges (it's finishing CI now). One check: do you want this **only** in `deploy/README.md`, or also mirrored as a dedicated page on the docs site nav? I'd default to `deploy/README.md` as the single source and link to it — say the word if you'd rather it be a first-class docs page.

## Before you deploy: production prerequisites

Read this before a production rollout. It's the "what must change, what access you
need, and what your cloud must provide" checklist; the [provisioning runbook](#one-time-provisioning)
below is the how.

### 1. What you must change (never ship the eval/dev defaults)

The prebuilt-image quickstart ([docs/getting-started](../docs/getting-started.md)) is a
**dev-bypass eval stack** — it disables auth, uses a passwordless DB, and binds to
loopback. A production deployment must flip all of the following. Values live in
[`deploy/.env.app.prod.example`](.env.app.prod.example) (app settings) +
[`deploy/terraform/variables.tf`](terraform/variables.tf) (infra):

| Setting | Eval default | Production |
|---|---|---|
| `AUTH_DEV_BYPASS` | `true` | **`false`** — this is the master auth switch; leaving it on means **no authentication at all**. |
| `AZURE_TENANT_ID` / `AZURE_API_CLIENT_ID` / `AZURE_SPA_CLIENT_ID` | empty | your Azure AD tenant + the two app registrations (API + SPA). |
| **Frontend build** | prebuilt `:dev` image (bypass baked in) | **rebuild the SPA from source with your `VITE_AZURE_*`** — Vite bakes auth config at build time, so no prebuilt image can carry your tenant (the published `:latest` has none baked and shows the "not configured" banner). See [frontend/Dockerfile](../frontend/Dockerfile). |
| `SECRET_STORE` | `redis` (eval) | **`azure_key_vault`** + `AZURE_KEY_VAULT_URL` + the managed identity's `AZURE_CLIENT_ID` (#408). |
| `DATABASE_URL` / `REDIS_URL` | inline, passwordless | Key Vault-backed Container Apps secrets — **never literals**; real credentials. |
| `CORS_ALLOW_ORIGINS` | n/a (same-origin) | empty **if** the SWA links the API same-origin; else the SPA origin. |
| `PUBLIC_BASE_URL` | n/a | the public origin (used to assemble webhook URLs). |
| `WORKSPACE_ADMIN_EMAILS` | seeded dev user | a **minimal** real allowlist — admins can read every suite's failing-row samples (see [Operational notes](#operational-notes)). |
| `APPLICATIONINSIGHTS_CONNECTION_STRING` | unset | your App Insights resource (observability). |
| `key_vault_purge_protection` (Terraform) | `false` (bring-up) | **`true`** for a hardened vault (irreversible). |
| Interactive API docs | served | **404 in prod** via the prod-docs gate (`ENVIRONMENT=prod`). |

### 2. Access you need

- **Azure subscription** — rights to create the resource group, Container Apps
  environment, PostgreSQL Flexible Server, Cache for Redis, Key Vault, Application
  Insights + Log Analytics, and the Static Web App (Contributor on the RG/subscription),
  **plus** `User Access Administrator`/`Owner` to grant the managed identity the **Key
  Vault Secrets User** role (an RBAC role assignment).
- **Azure AD (Entra ID)** — `Application Administrator` (or Global Admin) to create the
  **two app registrations** (API + SPA) and **grant admin consent** for the API scope.
- **Subscription resource-provider registration** — `Microsoft.App`,
  `Microsoft.DBforPostgreSQL`, `Microsoft.Cache`, `Microsoft.KeyVault`,
  `Microsoft.Insights`, `Microsoft.OperationalInsights`, `Microsoft.Web` (see
  [rp.tf](terraform/rp.tf)); needs subscription-level rights.
- **GitHub repo admin** — to set the Actions [secrets/vars](#github-config-the-workflow-reads)
  and create the OIDC **federated credential** (subject = the repo's `production`
  environment). The GHCR image push uses the built-in `GITHUB_TOKEN` (`packages: write`);
  the package must be **public** so Container Apps pulls it anonymously (ADR 0023).
- **Tooling** — Terraform + the `az` CLI, authenticated to the subscription.

### 3. Cloud prerequisites

DataQ is provider-agnostic by design — Azure is one target behind the app's seams
(ADR [0010](../docs/adr/0010-provider-agnostic-infrastructure-seams.md) /
[0013](../docs/adr/0013-marketplace-distribution-and-anti-lock-in.md)), so no cloud is
baked into app code. Today **Azure is the supported, implemented target**; AWS and GCP
are planned.

#### Azure — supported today

- An Azure **subscription** + a region with quota for **1 Container Apps environment**,
  **1 PostgreSQL Flexible Server**, Cache for Redis, Key Vault, App Insights + Log
  Analytics, and a Static Web App. (Free/trial tiers cap one ACA env + one Postgres
  server per subscription, so the app **shares** the RG/env/Postgres server with the
  harness and namespaces its own DB + role — ADR [0024](../docs/adr/0024-app-deployment-infrastructure.md).)
- The **resource providers** and **app registrations** from §2 registered/created.
- The **GHCR** backend package public. Then follow [One-time provisioning](#one-time-provisioning).

#### AWS — planned (not yet available)

Not yet implemented. The seams map to: ECS Fargate or App Runner (api + worker) · RDS
for PostgreSQL · ElastiCache for Redis · Secrets Manager (`SecretStore` impl) · CloudWatch
+ OpenTelemetry (observability) · Cognito or an OIDC IdP behind `get_current_user`. Track
via the anti-lock-in roadmap ([ADR 0013](../docs/adr/0013-marketplace-distribution-and-anti-lock-in.md)).

#### GCP — planned (not yet available)

Not yet implemented. The seams map to: Cloud Run (api + worker) · Cloud SQL for
PostgreSQL · Memorystore for Redis · Secret Manager (`SecretStore` impl) · Cloud Logging +
OpenTelemetry · Identity Platform / an OIDC IdP behind `get_current_user`.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# DataQ — deployment guide

How DataQ v1 is deployed to Azure. Infrastructure is **in-repo Terraform**
(`deploy/terraform/`, applied — [ADR 0024](../docs/adr/0024-app-deployment-infrastructure.md));
the app rolls out via the **`Deploy`** workflow
([.github/workflows/deploy.yml](../.github/workflows/deploy.yml), `workflow_dispatch`).
The stack is **live** — this is the runbook to provision a fresh environment and to
deploy a new image. Related: [ADR 0025](../docs/adr/0025-production-image-pip-slim.md)
(slim+pip image), [ADR 0023](../docs/adr/0023-container-image-registry-ghcr.md) (GHCR).

Azure is **one** deploy target behind the app's seams (ADR 0010/0013) — the
manifests here are infra config, not business logic. No Azure resource names are
hardcoded in app code; they live only as Terraform vars + workflow `vars`/`secrets`.

## Topology

```
Browser ─► Azure Static Web App (frontend/dist)
              │  /api/* proxied to the linked backend (same-origin → no CORS)
              ▼
        Azure Container Apps
          • dataq-api     (FastAPI image, external ingress :8000)
          • dataq-worker  (same image, `celery -A ... worker` + beat)
          • dataq-migrate (Container Apps Job: `alembic upgrade head`)
              │
              ├─► Azure Database for PostgreSQL (DATABASE_URL)
              ├─► Azure Cache for Redis        (REDIS_URL)
              ├─► Azure Key Vault              (SECRET_STORE=azure_key_vault, managed identity)
              └─► Application Insights         (APPLICATIONINSIGHTS_CONNECTION_STRING)
        GitHub Container Registry (GHCR) — holds the backend image (ADR 0023)
          ghcr.io/theurgicduke771/dataq-backend:<tag> — public package, so ACA
          pulls it anonymously (no registry credential stored on the apps/job).
```

Both api + worker run the **same** image ([backend/Dockerfile](../backend/Dockerfile),
build context = repo root). The frontend is built and uploaded to SWA by the
workflow (the [frontend/Dockerfile](../frontend/Dockerfile) is the container
alternative — only needed if you run the UI on Container Apps instead of SWA).

## One-time provisioning

The datasource + compute infra is stood up by the external Terraform harness
(ADR 0021) — see `HARNESS_TODO.md`. Beyond that, this app needs:

1. An **ACA environment** + the three apps/job above (the backend image is on
   **GHCR**, not ACR — ADR 0023). The api/worker run `uvicorn …` / `celery …`;
   the migrate **job** runs `alembic upgrade head`. The `deploy/terraform/` stack
   provisions all of this; the GHCR package must be **public** so ACA pulls it
   anonymously.
2. **Managed identity** on the api + worker apps with **Key Vault Secrets User**
   on the vault (so `DefaultAzureCredential` resolves `SECRET_STORE=azure_key_vault`).
3. **App env**: set the keys on the api + worker apps. The **complete** env-var
   reference (every Settings key) is [../.env.app.example](../.env.app.example);
   the prod-specific *values* are in [deploy/.env.app.prod.example](.env.app.prod.example).
   Secret values (DB/Redis URL, App Insights, webhook URLs) are Key Vault-backed
   Container Apps secrets — never literals. The user-assigned managed identity
   needs `AZURE_CLIENT_ID` set so `DefaultAzureCredential` resolves it (#408).
4. **SWA linked backend**: link the `dataq-api` Container App as the SWA backend so
   `/api/*` is proxied same-origin (then `CORS_ALLOW_ORIGINS` can stay empty). If
   instead the SPA calls the API cross-origin, set `CORS_ALLOW_ORIGINS` to the SWA
   origin (the FastAPI CORS middleware turns on only when it's non-empty).
5. **Azure Monitor → ADF webhook** alert rule (Week-7 task) — needs the deployed
   API URL; configure after the first deploy. Per [ADR 0006](../docs/adr/0006-adf-webhook-authentication.md)
   the shared secret rides the URL as a `?token=` query param, so don't
   hand-assemble it (wrong host / stale token after rotation / missing `?token=`
   are easy to get wrong — #92).

   **Easiest path: the in-app webhook-config surface (#490).** Sign in as a
   workspace admin → **Settings → Webhooks** to copy the ready-to-paste ADF
   URL (host + current `?token=` from Key Vault) and the Airflow URL. Set
   `PUBLIC_BASE_URL` so the generated host is the public origin (the deploy sets
   it to the SWA host; empty falls back to the request host). Paste the ADF URL
   into the Action Group webhook field. Note the **live ADF delivery still needs
   the Common-Alert-Schema payload mapping (#492)** — the alert body Azure Monitor
   sends differs from what the receiver parses today.

   Or build it from the CLI (the live host + Key Vault secret):

   ```bash
   # Vars you already set for the deploy workflow + the vault name.
   RG=<AZURE_RESOURCE_GROUP>; API_APP_NAME=<API_APP_NAME>; VAULT=<key-vault-name>
   API_HOST=$(az containerapp show -n "$API_APP_NAME" -g "$RG" \
     --query properties.configuration.ingress.fqdn -o tsv)
   # ADF_WEBHOOK_SECRET_NAME (default 'adf-webhook-secret') is the Key Vault *key*.
   TOKEN=$(az keyvault secret show --vault-name "$VAULT" --name adf-webhook-secret \
     --query value -o tsv)
   printf 'ADF webhook URL: https://%s/api/v1/orchestration/events/adf?token=%s\n' \
     "$API_HOST" "$TOKEN"
   ```

   ⚠️ The printed URL **contains the shared secret**. Paste it straight into the
   Action Group webhook config; never commit it, and don't run this where the
   output is captured to a log (CI, `script`, screen-share). The secret has a
   single source of truth (Key Vault), so re-run after a rotation
   ([ADR 0006](../docs/adr/0006-adf-webhook-authentication.md) is a hard cutover).

   The token is placed in the URL **un-encoded**, and the receiver compares the
   *URL-decoded* `token` against the Key Vault value — so the webhook secret must
   be **URL-safe** (generate it as e.g. `openssl rand -hex 32`). If an existing
   secret contains reserved characters (`+` `/` `=` `&` `#` space), percent-encode
   the token in the pasted URL, or it will silently fail auth (401).

   The **Airflow** callback URL is the sibling endpoint but carries **no secret**
   — it's HMAC-signed in a header ([ADR 0007](../docs/adr/0007-airflow-callback-model.md)),
   with the signing key configured in the DAG snippet ([integrations/airflow/](../integrations/airflow/)),
   not the URL — so it's just `https://$API_HOST/api/v1/orchestration/events/airflow`.

## GitHub config the workflow reads

Set under repo **Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions**, and add a
federated credential for OIDC login (subject = this repo's `production`
environment).

**Secrets:** `AZURE_CLIENT_ID`, `AZURE_TENANT_ID`, `AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID`
(OIDC app registration), `AZURE_STATIC_WEB_APPS_API_TOKEN`. The GHCR image push
uses the built-in `GITHUB_TOKEN` (`packages: write`) — no registry secret to set.

**Variables:** `AZURE_RESOURCE_GROUP`, `API_APP_NAME`, `WORKER_APP_NAME`,
`MIGRATE_JOB_NAME`, and the non-secret `VITE_AZURE_*` build values. (No `ACR_*` —
the image lives on GHCR at a fixed `ghcr.io/theurgicduke771/dataq-backend` path.)

## Going live

1. Provision the resources + set the secrets/vars above.
2. Run the **Deploy** workflow manually (`workflow_dispatch`) to validate end-to-end.
3. To deploy on every merge, uncomment the `push: branches: [main]` trigger in
   the workflow.

Migrations are additive/backward-compatible (CLAUDE.md), so the workflow runs
`alembic upgrade head` **before** rolling the apps — the running old code
tolerates the new schema. (This is exactly the dev-DB step that, when skipped,
500s the checks endpoint after a schema-adding deploy.)

Use an **immutable** `image_tag` per release — ACA caches a tag at the node, so a
same-tag rebuild won't be re-pulled on a new revision. Push-on-merge is
intentionally **off**; deploys are manual `workflow_dispatch`.

## Verify

- `GET /healthz` → `200 {"status":"ok"}`.
- `GET /api/v1/me` → **401** (auth enforced; a valid token resolves the user).
- SPA root + a deep link → 200; sign-in via Azure AD SSO.
- Interactive API docs (`/docs`, `/redoc`, `/openapi.json`) are **404 in prod**
  (#170 — the prod-docs gate); the MCP server is mounted at `/mcp` (Azure-AD
  validated; fail-closed without auth — [ADR 0008](../docs/adr/0008-mcp-server.md)).
- Celery beat starts clean (no `NoneType` lock crash, #405/#407) and orchestration
  polling reads Key Vault secrets (#406/#408).

## Operational notes

- **Restart dependent Container Apps after a shared-Postgres delete/recreate** —
  the DB host is injected as a start-time secret snapshot, so every dependent
  revision must be restarted or it keeps resolving the old/dead host.
- The shared RG / Container Apps env / Postgres server are **reused, never
  destroyed** (free/trial caps one of each; shared with the harness — ADR 0024).
- `key_vault_purge_protection` is off during bring-up (so a destroy/re-apply can
  reuse the vault name); set it **true** for a hardened prod (irreversible).
- **Workspace-admins are superusers over every suite** ([ADR 0027](../docs/adr/0027-suite-permission-model-workspace-admin.md) / #482):
  anyone in `WORKSPACE_ADMIN_EMAILS` can read **all** suites' results — including
  failing-row samples (`results.sample_failures`), the one place PII/PHI lands —
  and manage/delete any suite. **Keep the allowlist minimal.** For a **PHI / regulated
  deployment**, treat the data-access audit trail (G1 / #431 in
  [compliance-posture.md](../docs/compliance-posture.md)) as a **prerequisite before
  granting broad workspace-admin** — PHI is already G1-blocked, and this access breadth
  raises the bar.

lines: manifests here are infra config, not business logic. No Azure resource names are
hardcoded in app code; they live only as Terraform vars + workflow `vars`/`secrets`.
+Read this before a production rollout. It's the "what must change, what access you
+need, and what your cloud must provide" checklist; the [provisioning runbook](#one-time-provisioning)
+### 1. What you must change (never ship the eval/dev defaults)
+**dev-bypass eval stack** — it disables auth, uses a passwordless DB, and binds to
+loopback. A production deployment must flip all of the following. Values live in
+| `AUTH_DEV_BYPASS` | `true` | **`false`** — this is the master auth switch; leaving it on means **no authentication at all**. |
+| `AZURE_TENANT_ID` / `AZURE_API_CLIENT_ID` / `AZURE_SPA_CLIENT_ID` | empty | your Azure AD tenant + the two app registrations (API + SPA). |

+| **Frontend build** | prebuilt `:dev` image (bypass baked in) | **rebuild the SPA from source with your `VITE_AZURE_*`** — Vite bakes auth config at build time, so no prebuilt image can carry your tenant (the published `:latest` has none baked and shows the "not configured" banner). See [frontend/Dockerfile](../frontend/Dockerfile). |
+| `DATABASE_URL` / `REDIS_URL` | inline, passwordless | Key Vault-backed Container Apps secrets — **never literals**; real credentials. |
+| `CORS_ALLOW_ORIGINS` | n/a (same-origin) | empty **if** the SWA links the API same-origin; else the SPA origin. |
+| `PUBLIC_BASE_URL` | n/a | the public origin (used to assemble webhook URLs). |
+| `WORKSPACE_ADMIN_EMAILS` | seeded dev user | a **minimal** real allowlist — admins can read every suite's failing-row samples (see [Operational notes](#operational-notes)). |
+| Interactive API docs | served | **404 in prod** via the prod-docs gate (`ENVIRONMENT=prod`). |
+- **Azure subscription** — rights to create the resource group, Container Apps
+  environment, PostgreSQL Flexible Server, Cache for Redis, Key Vault, Application
+  Insights + Log Analytics, and the Static Web App (Contributor on the RG/subscription),
+  **plus** `User Access Administrator`/`Owner` to grant the managed identity the **Key
+  Vault Secrets User** role (an RBAC role assignment).
+- **Azure AD (Entra ID)** — `Application Administrator` (or Global Admin) to create the
+  **two app registrations** (API + SPA) and **grant admin consent** for the API scope.
+- **GitHub repo admin** — to set the Actions [secrets/vars](#github-config-the-workflow-reads)
+  and create the OIDC **federated credential** (subject = the repo's `production`
+  environment). The GHCR image push uses the built-in `GITHUB_TOKEN` (`packages: write`);
+  the package must be **public** so Container Apps pulls it anonymously (ADR 0023).
+- **Tooling** — Terraform + the `az` CLI, authenticated to the subscription.
+DataQ is provider-agnostic by design — Azure is one target behind the app's seams
+baked into app code. Today **Azure is the supported, implemented target**; AWS and GCP
+- An Azure **subscription** + a region with quota for **1 Container Apps environment**,
+  **1 PostgreSQL Flexible Server**, Cache for Redis, Key Vault, App Insights + Log
+  Analytics, and a Static Web App. (Free/trial tiers cap one ACA env + one Postgres
+  server per subscription, so the app **shares** the RG/env/Postgres server with the
+  harness and namespaces its own DB + role — ADR [0024](../docs/adr/0024-app-deployment-infrastructure.md).)
+- The **resource providers** and **app registrations** from §2 registered/created.
+- The **GHCR** backend package public. Then follow [One-time provisioning](#one-time-provisioning).
+Not yet implemented. The seams map to: ECS Fargate or App Runner (api + worker) · RDS
+for PostgreSQL · ElastiCache for Redis · Secrets Manager (`SecretStore` impl) · CloudWatch
++ OpenTelemetry (observability) · Cognito or an OIDC IdP behind `get_current_user`. Track
+Not yet implemented. The seams map to: Cloud Run (api + worker) · Cloud SQL for
+PostgreSQL · Memorystore for Redis · Secret Manager (`SecretStore` impl) · Cloud Logging +
+OpenTelemetry · Identity Platform / an OIDC IdP behind `get_current_user`.

file.content:
_mcp_app = build_mcp_app()
_lifespan = combine_lifespans(lifespan, _mcp_app.lifespan) if _mcp_app is not None else lifespan


def docs_kwargs(settings: Settings) -> dict[str, str | None]:
    """FastAPI doc-exposure kwargs, gated by environment (#170 — prod-docs gate).

    The interactive docs (`/docs`, `/redoc`) and the raw OpenAPI schema
    (`/openapi.json`) are OFF in production — don't publish the full API surface
    on the public ingress — and ON in dev/staging for developer convenience.
    Disabling `openapi_url` also disables both doc UIs (they fetch it), so all
    three ride the one environment check.
    """
    enabled = settings.environment != "prod"
    return {
        "docs_url": "/docs" if enabled else None,
        "redoc_url": "/redoc" if enabled else None,
        "openapi_url": "/openapi.json" if enabled else None,
    }

content.text: Hey. Are we overcomplicating the things, or this is how it is for BYOL projects?

lastPrompt: Hey. Are we overcomplicating the things, or this is how it is for BYOL projects?

content.text:
Honest answer: **~80% of it is just what BYOL work is, and ~20% is one genuine complexity smell we inherited** — not something this task invented.

**The standard, unavoidable parts** (every self-hosted/BYOL project does these):
- Multi-arch images — table stakes once you distribute images.
- A pull-based compose that migrates + seeds — *the* canonical "try it in 5 min" pattern.
- A same-origin reverse proxy so the SPA and API share an origin without a cloud load balancer — standard for shipping an SPA+API together.
- Splitting "run from image" vs "build from source" docs — normal.

None of that is over-engineering; it's the price of "someone else runs this on their machine."

**The one real smell — the two frontend images (`:latest` + `:dev`).** That exists *only* because the SPA bakes its auth config into the bundle at build time (Vite inlines `VITE_*`), and dev-bypass is tied to `import.meta.env.DEV`. That single design choice is what forced: two images, the awkward "`:latest` shows an unconfigured banner as-pulled," and the `NODE_ENV=development` trick. That's not inherent to BYOL — it's a build-time-config decision leaking into distribution.

**How mature self-hosted SPAs avoid it:** make auth config *runtime*, not build-time. The container serves a tiny `/config.json` (or `window.__ENV`) that nginx fills from env at startup, and the SPA reads it at boot. Then **one** image works for both the eval demo *and* a real self-hoster's Azure AD — they just pass their tenant as env vars at `docker run`, no rebuild. And here's the kicker: **we already built the mechanism** — the nginx `envsubst`-at-startup we added for `DATAQ_API_UPSTREAM` is exactly what would inject that config. So collapsing two images into one is a small, natural follow-up, not a rewrite.

**Verdict:** for a single-tenant, Azure-first v1 that's 8 weeks in, what we shipped is proportionate and well-documented — I wouldn't unwind it now. But if BYOL becomes a real distribution channel post-v1, the **runtime-frontend-config** change is the one simplification worth making: it deletes the second image, the "unusable `:latest`" sharp edge, and the docs caveats that go with them.

Also worth separating: the *process* volume (3 PRs, reviews, CI waits) isn't overcomplication — that's this repo's own working agreements (one-functionality commits, review-before-merge). That's discipline you've chosen, not overhead I added.

Want me to file that runtime-config idea as a post-v1 issue so it's captured against `context/post-v1-roadmap.md`?

content.text: How much effort is that to make the change now?

Let me ground the estimate in the actual code rather than hand-wave — the effort hinges on whether the app reads auth config synchronously at import (easy to redirect) or would need an async refactor.

input.command:
cd /Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ/frontend
echo "=== index.html ==="; cat index.html
echo "=== main.tsx (bootstrap order) ==="; sed -n '1,45p' src/main.tsx
echo "=== who imports authConfig/authMode (synchronous consumers) ==="
rg -l "authConfig|authMode" src | head
echo "=== config.test.ts size ==="; wc -l tests/auth/config.test.ts
input.description: Inspect bootstrap + config consumers to size the runtime-config change

lastPrompt: How much effort is that to make the change now?

content.text:
Good news: the code is shaped so this is a **half-day change, one PR** — not a refactor. The reason it's cheap is that `authConfig`/`authMode` are computed **synchronously at module load** and `index.html` loads the app as a deferred module script. So I can inject a **synchronous global before the bundle runs** and nothing downstream changes.

**The approach** (reuses the envsubst we already built — no new moving parts):
- `index.html` gets a blocking `<script src="/config.js">` in `<head>` (runs before the deferred module bundle).
- nginx serves `/config.js` from a `location = /config.js { return 200 'window.__DATAQ_CONFIG__={...}' }` in the **already-envsubst'd** `nginx.conf.template`, filled from `${DATAQ_AZURE_*}` env at container start.
- `config.ts` reads `window.__DATAQ_CONFIG__` first, falls back to `import.meta.env` for `pnpm dev`. Its six consumers don't change at all.

**Effort, file by file (~4–5 hrs):**

| Work | Effort |
|---|---|
| `config.ts` read window-global + `window.d.ts` type | ~30 min |
| `index.html` config script + dev fallback | ~20 min |
| `nginx.conf.template` `/config.js` location + Dockerfile ENV defaults | ~30 min |
| **Delete** the two-image machinery: Dockerfile `VITE_*`/`BUILD_NODE_ENV` args, the `:dev` variant in `publish-images.yml`, compose → single `:latest` + env | ~45 min |
| Rewrite `config.test.ts` (62 lines) to drive the runtime global | ~45 min |
| Docs: remove the `:latest`-unconfigured / two-image caveats | ~30 min |
| Local verify (one image, dev-bypass **and** simulated real-auth via env) + review + PR + CI | ~60 min |

**What it buys:** one frontend image instead of two, the `NODE_ENV=development` trick gone, the "`:latest` shows an unconfigured banner" sharp edge gone, real self-host becomes "set env vars at `docker run`" (no rebuild), and the docs shrink.

**The one caveat you should weigh:** this moves dev-bypass from build-time to runtime, which effectively **reverses the decision you made earlier** (you chose "two tags" *over* relaxing the `import.meta.env.DEV` guard). It's cleaner here — bypass is an explicit operator env var and the backend still enforces auth regardless — but it does remove that build-time guardrail, so it's a conscious re-decision, and it'll trip the same security-review flag we hit on the compose (mitigated the same way: the image is safe, the operator opts into bypass).

My take: it's genuinely worth doing and cheap, but it's **not urgent for an Azure-first v1** — the current thing works and is documented. If you're planning to lean on BYOL post-v1, I'd do it then (or now if you want the distribution story clean before v1 ships). Want me to do it now, or file it against `context/post-v1-roadmap.md`?

content.text: Also, keep in mind that it should be accommodating to multi cloud plus local. We don't wanted to make the image or config specific to any one cloud provider.

That constraint actually fits this change well — and it sharpens what belongs *in* it versus what's a separate, larger piece. Let me be precise about the boundary, because "cloud-neutral" has two layers here:

**Layer 1 — the image + config mechanism (this change delivers it, cleanly):**
The whole point of runtime config injection is that **the image carries zero cloud specifics** — everything comes from env at `docker run`. So one generic image runs on Azure, AWS, GCP, or a laptop; only the injected values differ. To make it *properly* neutral rather than "Azure values injected," I'd shape the config as a **generic identity contract**, not Azure fields:

```js
window.__DATAQ_CONFIG__ = {
  auth: {
    mode: "bypass" | "oidc",        // "bypass" = local/eval, no IdP
    provider: "azure_ad",           // impl selector (only one wired in v1)
    authority: "https://login.microsoftonline.com/<tenant>/v2.0",
    clientId: "…",
    apiScope: "…",
  }
}
```

Env vars become `DATAQ_AUTH_MODE` / `DATAQ_AUTH_AUTHORITY` / `DATAQ_AUTH_CLIENT_ID` / `DATAQ_AUTH_API_SCOPE` / `DATAQ_AUTH_PROVIDER` — **no `AZURE` in the image or the contract**. Azure is just one populated shape; the fields map cleanly onto MSAL (`authority`, `clientId`, `scope`) so nothing is contorted. This is consistent with your existing anti-lock-in seams (ADR 0010/0013 — a generic seam, Azure as the one impl). Cost: **+1–2 hrs** on top of the half-day.

**Layer 2 — actually authenticating against AWS/GCP IdPs (NOT this change):**
The frontend's auth *library* is **MSAL**, which is Azure-AD-specific. A generic config contract lets you *express* a Cognito/Identity-Platform/Keycloak authority, but MSAL won't talk to them. Real non-Azure sign-in needs a **generic OIDC client** (e.g. `oidc-client-ts`) behind an auth-provider seam — that's the larger, separate effort that lines up with the AWS/GCP "planned" status. I'd **not** build it now; the config contract just leaves the door open so no future image change is needed.

- **Backend** is already neutral at the boundary (`get_current_user`, no Entra-claim reads — CLAUDE.md §11), so it's ready.
- **Local** is trivially neutral: `mode: "bypass"` needs no IdP at all.

So the honest scope: **this change makes the package and config genuinely cloud-neutral (one image, generic contract, ~half a day + ~1–2 hrs)** and delivers Azure + local out of the box. It does **not** make non-Azure IdPs work — that's the deferred OIDC-client seam, and I'd file it as the post-v1 companion.

Want me to **do the cloud-neutral runtime-config change now** (one image, generic `DATAQ_AUTH_*` contract, Azure+bypass working) and **file the generic-OIDC-client seam** against `context/post-v1-roadmap.md`? Or file both and hold?

content.text: Also, we need to make the deep bypass off by default. It has to be explicitly flip in order to be enabled.

lastPrompt: Also, we need to make the deep bypass off by default. It has to be explicitly flip in order to be enabled.

content.text:
That's the right call, and it actually makes the whole thing *more* secure than what we have today. Here's how it lands:

**Bypass becomes fail-closed by construction.** In the runtime-config model the image bakes *nothing* — so there's no bypass baked into any published artifact. Auth mode resolves like this, with bypass never a fallback:

```
mode = window.__DATAQ_CONFIG__.auth.mode          // injected at runtime
- unset / anything but "bypass"  → real auth (oidc) if authority+clientId present, else "unconfigured" banner
- exactly "bypass"               → dev-bypass  (the ONLY way in)
```

So a plain `docker run` with no env → **not** bypassed (shows "unconfigured", backend returns 401). Bypass turns on **only** when the operator explicitly sets `DATAQ_AUTH_MODE=bypass` — and our eval compose is the one place that does the explicit flip (alongside the backend's `AUTH_DEV_BYPASS=true`, which already defaults to `False`). Both layers off-by-default, both explicitly opted in only for the eval stack.

**Why this is a net security win:** today's published `:dev` image is literally an auth-disabled build sitting on a public registry — pull it, deploy it, you've got no auth. The runtime model **deletes that artifact entirely**; nothing shippable has bypass baked, so the "off by default, explicit flip" property is structural, not a convention.

**Consolidated plan** (all three of your constraints):
1. **One cloud-neutral image** — config injected at runtime via nginx-envsubst'd `/config.js` (no cloud, no bypass baked).
2. **Generic `DATAQ_AUTH_*` contract** (`mode` / `provider` / `authority` / `clientId` / `apiScope`) — Azure is one populated shape via MSAL; the OIDC client for non-Azure IdPs is the deferred post-v1 seam.
3. **Bypass off by default, explicit `mode=bypass` to enable** — never inferred; verified by testing that a config-less image is *not* bypassed.

**Effort:** ~half a day + ~1–2 hrs for the generic schema ≈ **one PR, ~5–6 hrs**. I'll explicitly test the fail-closed property and expect (and pre-mitigate) the security-review flag.

Two things I'd still file rather than build now: the **generic OIDC client** (real AWS/GCP/Keycloak sign-in) and — worth confirming — do you want me to **keep the current `:dev` image working until this lands**, or is it fine that this PR removes it and the eval compose switches to `:latest` + `DATAQ_AUTH_MODE=bypass` in the same change?

Green light to implement, or want to adjust anything first?

content.text: Can we check this generic OIDC client with Azure as well?

Yes — and that's actually the smartest way to de-risk it, because it could collapse the whole thing to **one auth client for every provider including Azure**.

**Why it works:** Azure AD is a standards-compliant OIDC provider. Its authority (`https://login.microsoftonline.com/<tenant>/v2.0`) exposes the normal discovery doc, and the SPA flow (authorization code + PKCE) is vanilla OIDC. So a generic client like `oidc-client-ts` can authenticate against Azure just as it would against Cognito, GCP Identity Platform, or Keycloak. If it holds up, you get **one auth stack for Azure + AWS + GCP + local**, no MSAL, and the config contract simplifies to just `mode: bypass | oidc` + `authority`/`clientId`/`apiScope` — you don't even need a `provider` discriminator.

**The catch — it means replacing MSAL, which is the path that's live in production right now.** So this isn't free:
- The token work we just shipped (#168 `InteractionRequiredAuthError` fallback) and the silent-refresh in the interceptor are MSAL-specific; they'd be redone in `oidc-client-ts` terms (it has its own silent-renew + renew-error → redirect handling).
- You trade MSAL's Azure-tuned conveniences for standard OIDC.

**What the spike must actually prove (this is the whole risk):** the login redirect is vanilla OIDC and will "just work" — the Azure-specific behavior lives in **acquiring an access token for your API scope** (`api://<api-client-id>/user_impersonation`) and **silent renew**. That's the exact thing to verify against the real tenant. If the generic client cleanly gets that API-scoped token and renews it silently → **retire MSAL, one client for all**. If Azure throws a quirk that needs MSAL → fall back to the seam (MSAL for Azure + OIDC for others, two impls).

**So the recommendation for the deferred issue:** frame it as *"generic OIDC client, validated against Azure first — if the API-scope token + silent renew are clean, drop MSAL entirely."* That's the outcome you want (one stack), and checking Azure up front is what decides it.

None of this changes the immediate move — the runtime-config / one-image / bypass-off-by-default change stands on its own, and the generic `DATAQ_AUTH_*` contract we're adding makes *either* auth outcome a drop-in later (no image change needed).

So, to lock scope: I'll **do the runtime-config change now** (cloud-neutral image, generic contract, bypass fail-closed), and **file the OIDC issue as "validate against Azure first → retire MSAL if clean, else two-impl seam."** Green light?

lastPrompt: Can we check this generic OIDC client with Azure as well?

content.text: So what is your final verdict on multi vendor, multi architecture, generic container image?

# Final verdict

**All three are the right goal — but they sit at three different maturity stages, and only two are "now." The discipline that keeps this from becoming over-engineering is: build the *seams* now, defer the second *implementations* until there's a real second target.**

### 1. Multi-architecture — ✅ Done, non-negotiable
`amd64` + `arm64` is already shipped and verified on GHCR. Cheap, standard, zero downside. Nothing more to do.

### 2. Generic container image — ✅ Do it now
One image per component, **nothing baked in** — no cloud, no secrets, no auth-bypass. Config injected at runtime (`/config.js` via the nginx envsubst we already have). This is ~½ day and is a **strict improvement** even for an Azure-only v1:
- Deletes the two-image split, the `NODE_ENV` trick, and the "`:latest` shows unconfigured" edge.
- **Removes the auth-disabled `:dev` artifact from a public registry** — bypass becomes structurally fail-closed (off by default, explicit `mode=bypass` only).
- Real self-host becomes "set env vars," not "rebuild."

There is no good reason to keep cloud/bypass baked into an image. Do this.

### 3. Multi-vendor — 🟡 Seam now, implementation later
Split it honestly into two layers:

- **The neutral surface (now, ~free):** the generic image + a provider-shaped `DATAQ_AUTH_*` config contract, on top of the backend boundaries you already have (`get_current_user`, `SecretStore`, `ResultPublisher`, `OrchestrationProvider`). After this, **the image and contract are genuinely cloud-neutral** — Azure is just the one populated shape, and moving to another vendor needs **no image change**.
- **Actual AWS/GCP (post-v1, real work):** a **generic OIDC client validated against Azure first** (if the API-scope token + silent renew are clean, retire MSAL → one auth stack for all; else MSAL-for-Azure + OIDC-for-others), **plus** AWS/GCP Terraform. Neither exists today; both are tracked, not built.

### The guardrail (this is the anti-overcomplication rule)
**Do not build the OIDC client or AWS/GCP IaC now.** For an Azure-first v1, that's speculative — a seam with one impl is correct; a *second impl* with no second target is the over-engineering you asked about at the start. The generic contract keeps the door open at ~zero cost; walking through it waits for a real customer/target.

### Bottom line
> **One multi-arch image per component, nothing baked in, config + auth + secrets injected at runtime behind generic seams, bypass fail-closed, Azure as the single wired implementation.** That end-state is fully cloud-neutral *in packaging and contract* today, and makes genuine AWS/GCP support a drop-in later — without paying for a second implementation before you need it.

Concretely: **do the runtime-config / one-image / bypass-off-by-default change now** (cloud-neutral, ~½ day + ~1–2 hrs for the generic contract), and **file two post-v1 issues** — the Azure-validated generic OIDC client, and AWS/GCP deploy IaC. That's the proportionate path.

Want me to start the implementation and file those two issues?

Clear directives. Let me take them in order — but first let me address your **Azure rebuild question**, because it interacts with the design:

**Short answer: yes, and it's actually the natural way to adopt this.** The catch worth knowing up front: the runtime-config model serves `/config.js` from the **nginx frontend container**, but the deployed app currently serves the UI from **Azure Static Web App** (static files, no runtime injection). So adopting the generic image means the frontend moves **SWA → a Container App**. That makes a clean "remove the DataQ app, keep everything else" rebuild the *right* approach: keep Key Vault, App Insights/Log Analytics, the shared Postgres + `dataq` DB, and the Container Apps env; tear down and re-provision the app compute (api + worker + migrate job) **and replace the SWA with a frontend container app**. content.content:
=== ADR README head + last rows ===
# Architecture Decision Records (ADRs)

Each ADR captures a single significant architecture decision: the context, the decision, the consequences, and the alternatives considered. New ADRs are append-only — supersede an old decision by adding a new ADR and marking the old one's status as `Superseded by ADR-NNNN`.

## Format

- File name: `NNNN-short-kebab-slug.md` (zero-padded 4-digit sequence)
- Frontmatter fields:
  - **Status** — one of `Proposed`, `Accepted`, `Deprecated`, `Superseded by ADR-NNNN` (title-case)
  - **Date** — `YYYY-MM-DD`
  - **Deciders** — who made the call
  - **Consulted** *(optional)* — stakeholders whose sign-off the decision needed (e.g. product owner for ADR 0005). Omit when none.
  - **Supersedes** *(optional)* — `ADR-NNNN` this decision replaces. Omit when none.
  - **Superseded by** *(optional)* — `ADR-NNNN` that later replaced this one. Add when the status flips to `Superseded by`.
- Sections: Context, Decision, Consequences, Alternatives considered, Related (optional)
- Keep each ADR short — 1–2 pages. If it grows past that, the decision is probably two decisions.

### Template

```markdown
...
| [0019](0019-custom-sql-check-kind.md) | Custom-SQL checks ride `kind='expectation'` via GX `UnexpectedRowsExpectation` (no new kind); read-only validation + SQL-datasource gating | Accepted |
| [0020](0020-history-and-audit-strategy.md) | History/audit: per-entity Type-4 snapshot tables (`check_versions`, `connection_versions`) where config history is needed; no SCD-2; credentials never snapshotted; cascade-delete accepted; cross-entity audit log deferred | Accepted |
| [0021](0021-demo-test-data-environment-strategy.md) | Live test/demo-data environment (retail model, 3 reference flows) lives outside the repo — Terraform/mock-data/Databricks notebook not git-tracked; discharges the deferred live-warehouse/file smoke | Accepted |
| [0022](0022-week6-prototype-adoption-and-chart-library.md) | Week-6 prototype adoption — full 13-screen set as dedicated pages (Share is the only drawer; prototype wins on conflicts; Settings/Admin pulled into W6); chart library = recharts (lazy-loaded) | Accepted |
| [0023](0023-container-image-registry-ghcr.md) | Container image registry — GitHub Container Registry (GHCR) over ACR / Docker Hub (vendor-neutral, public package → anonymous ACA pull, reuses `GITHUB_TOKEN`); supersedes the #379 ACR scaffolding | Accepted |
| [0024](0024-app-deployment-infrastructure.md) | App deploy infra — in-repo Terraform; shares subscription/RG/ACA-env/Postgres with the harness; ACA api+worker+migrate-job + SWA-Standard linked same-origin `/api` + self-hosted Redis + KV (UAMI) + App Insights + AAD-app-reg OIDC | Accepted |
| [0025](0025-production-image-pip-slim.md) | Production image — multi-stage `python:3.13-slim` + pip (not conda; ~2.84GB→~1GB); conda stays the local-dev tool; amends the W1 conda lock | Accepted |
| [0027](0027-suite-permission-model-workspace-admin.md) | Suite permissions — workspace-admin is implicit `admin` on every suite (governance/break-glass); drop grantable suite-admin; normal users get owner/edit/view; workspace-admin gets workspace-wide visibility (supersedes #411/#412) | Accepted |

## Pending (to be written in their respective weeks)

| # | Topic | Target week |
|---|---|---|
=== ADR 0026 (recent, for format) ===
# ADR 0026 — DataQ-issued API keys / service tokens behind the auth seam (REST + MCP)

- **Status:** Proposed
- **Date:** 2026-06-29
- **Deciders:** @TheurgicDuke771
- **Related:** ADR [0010](0010-provider-agnostic-infrastructure-seams.md) (the `get_current_user` identity seam — Azure is one impl, not the architecture), [0013](0013-marketplace-distribution-and-anti-lock-in.md) (BYOL / anti-lock-in), [0008](0008-mcp-server.md) (MCP auth via `JWTVerifier` — bring-your-own-token today), [0020](0020-history-and-audit-strategy.md) (audit), compliance posture (#436)
- **Issue:** [#461](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/461)

> **Stub — Proposed, not yet designed in full.** Captures the direction while it's fresh; to be fleshed out when Theme 3 (access/identity) is picked up post-v1.

## Context

Authentication today is **exclusively Azure AD bearer tokens** (delegated/SSO) for both the REST API and `/mcp`. There is no DataQ-native credential. Two problems follow:

1. **Lock-in.** This is the deepest remaining Azure coupling. The `get_current_user` seam (ADR 0010) exists and service code never reads Entra claims (CLAUDE.md §11), but the seam has exactly *one* real implementation, and the persistence layer is still Azure-shaped (`users.aad_object_id`). A BYOL customer on AWS/GCP (ADR 0013) has no Azure AD and cannot authenticate at all.
2. **No programmatic access.** Headless agents, CI, and always-on MCP clients want a long-lived, scoped, revocable secret — not a ~60-min Azure token they must refresh (ADR 0008 uses a token-*validating* `JWTVerifier`, not a client-driven OAuth provider). There is no API-key / PAT / service-token mechanism.

## Decision (direction — to be detailed)

Introduce a **DataQ-issued credential as a second authenticator behind the existing `get_current_user` seam**, so the **REST API and `/mcp` accept it identically** — explicitly *not* an MCP-only feature. On MCP, fastmcp `MultiAuth` lets `/mcp` accept either a DataQ key or an Azure token. Adding a second real auth impl is also what *exercises* (and thereby validates) the seam ADR 0010 describes.

**Phased:**

1. **User-scoped PATs first.** A key belongs to a user and inherits that user's per-suite grants → **no new authz model** (reuse the `view < edit < admin < owner` ladder; optionally down-scope a key to read-only — ideal for MCP read tools / dashboards, smallest blast radius). ~80% of the value for a fraction of the change.
2. **Service-account principals later.** These force the larger move and should be sequenced after PATs prove the seam: generalize `users.aad_object_id` into a generic **principal** with pluggable identity bindings (Azure AD oid, API key, …), and extend suite **sharing** to non-user principals.

**Credential bar (this is a new secret surface — not to be half-built):**

- Hash-at-rest (argon2/bcrypt), **show-once** on creation, identifying prefix (`dq_live_…`), expiry defaults, revocation, last-used + audit.
- Hashes live in a **new `api_keys` table** — *not* the credential SecretStore (that store is for *retrievable* connection secrets; a key hash is a verifier secret, never retrievable).
- **Lifecycle tied to the owner**: deactivating a user kills their keys, so a PAT can't become an SSO-policy backdoor that outlives the account.
- Never logged (prefix only) — same discipline as the compliance posture (#436).

## Consequences (anticipated)

- Discharges the identity-seam lock-in (ADR 0010/0013): Azure AD becomes *one* authenticator behind the seam, not the only one — unblocking BYOL on non-Azure clouds.
- Closes the programmatic-access gap for REST automation and headless MCP clients without weakening the per-user authz model (PAT phase).
- Adds a credential-management surface (issue/list/revoke UI + API) and an audit obligation.

## Open questions (for the full ADR)

=== ADR README head + last rows ===
# Architecture Decision Records (ADRs)

Each ADR captures a single significant architecture decision: the context, the decision, the consequences, and the alternatives considered. New ADRs are append-only — supersede an old decision by adding a new ADR and marking the old one's status as `Superseded by ADR-NNNN`.

## Format

- File name: `NNNN-short-kebab-slug.md` (zero-padded 4-digit sequence)
- Frontmatter fields:
  - **Status** — one of `Proposed`, `Accepted`, `Deprecated`, `Superseded by ADR-NNNN` (title-case)
  - **Date** — `YYYY-MM-DD`
  - **Deciders** — who made the call
  - **Consulted** *(optional)* — stakeholders whose sign-off the decision needed (e.g. product owner for ADR 0005). Omit when none.
  - **Supersedes** *(optional)* — `ADR-NNNN` this decision replaces. Omit when none.
  - **Superseded by** *(optional)* — `ADR-NNNN` that later replaced this one. Add when the status flips to `Superseded by`.
- Sections: Context, Decision, Consequences, Alternatives considered, Related (optional)
- Keep each ADR short — 1–2 pages. If it grows past that, the decision is probably two decisions.

### Template

```markdown
...
| [0019](0019-custom-sql-check-kind.md) | Custom-SQL checks ride `kind='expectation'` via GX `UnexpectedRowsExpectation` (no new kind); read-only validation + SQL-datasource gating | Accepted |
| [0020](0020-history-and-audit-strategy.md) | History/audit: per-entity Type-4 snapshot tables (`check_versions`, `connection_versions`) where config history is needed; no SCD-2; credentials never snapshotted; cascade-delete accepted; cross-entity audit log deferred | Accepted |
| [0021](0021-demo-test-data-environment-strategy.md) | Live test/demo-data environment (retail model, 3 reference flows) lives outside the repo — Terraform/mock-data/Databricks notebook not git-tracked; discharges the deferred live-warehouse/file smoke | Accepted |
| [0022](0022-week6-prototype-adoption-and-chart-library.md) | Week-6 prototype adoption — full 13-screen set as dedicated pages (Share is the only drawer; prototype wins on conflicts; Settings/Admin pulled into W6); chart library = recharts (lazy-loaded) | Accepted |
| [0023](0023-container-image-registry-ghcr.md) | Container image registry — GitHub Container Registry (GHCR) over ACR / Docker Hub (vendor-neutral, public package → anonymous ACA pull, reuses `GITHUB_TOKEN`); supersedes the #379 ACR scaffolding | Accepted |
| [0024](0024-app-deployment-infrastructure.md) | App deploy infra — in-repo Terraform; shares subscription/RG/ACA-env/Postgres with the harness; ACA api+worker+migrate-job + SWA-Standard linked same-origin `/api` + self-hosted Redis + KV (UAMI) + App Insights + AAD-app-reg OIDC | Accepted |
| [0025](0025-production-image-pip-slim.md) | Production image — multi-stage `python:3.13-slim` + pip (not conda; ~2.84GB→~1GB); conda stays the local-dev tool; amends the W1 conda lock | Accepted |
| [0027](0027-suite-permission-model-workspace-admin.md) | Suite permissions — workspace-admin is implicit `admin` on every suite (governance/break-glass); drop grantable suite-admin; normal users get owner/edit/view; workspace-admin gets workspace-wide visibility (supersedes #411/#412) | Accepted |

|---|---|---|

# ADR 0026 — DataQ-issued API keys / service tokens behind the auth seam (REST + MCP)

- **Status:** Proposed
- **Date:** 2026-06-29
- **Deciders:** @TheurgicDuke771
- **Related:** ADR [0010](0010-provider-agnostic-infrastructure-seams.md) (the `get_current_user` identity seam — Azure is one impl, not the architecture), [0013](0013-marketplace-distribution-and-anti-lock-in.md) (BYOL / anti-lock-in), [0008](0008-mcp-server.md) (MCP auth via `JWTVerifier` — bring-your-own-token today), [0020](0020-history-and-audit-strategy.md) (audit), compliance posture (#436)
- **Issue:** [#461](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/461)

> **Stub — Proposed, not yet designed in full.** Captures the direction while it's fresh; to be fleshed out when Theme 3 (access/identity) is picked up post-v1.

## Context

Authentication today is **exclusively Azure AD bearer tokens** (delegated/SSO) for both the REST API and `/mcp`. There is no DataQ-native credential. Two problems follow:

1. **Lock-in.** This is the deepest remaining Azure coupling. The `get_current_user` seam (ADR 0010) exists and service code never reads Entra claims (CLAUDE.md §11), but the seam has exactly *one* real implementation, and the persistence layer is still Azure-shaped (`users.aad_object_id`). A BYOL customer on AWS/GCP (ADR 0013) has no Azure AD and cannot authenticate at all.
2. **No programmatic access.** Headless agents, CI, and always-on MCP clients want a long-lived, scoped, revocable secret — not a ~60-min Azure token they must refresh (ADR 0008 uses a token-*validating* `JWTVerifier`, not a client-driven OAuth provider). There is no API-key / PAT / service-token mechanism.

## Decision (direction — to be detailed)

Introduce a **DataQ-issued credential as a second authenticator behind the existing `get_current_user` seam**, so the **REST API and `/mcp` accept it identically** — explicitly *not* an MCP-only feature. On MCP, fastmcp `MultiAuth` lets `/mcp` accept either a DataQ key or an Azure token. Adding a second real auth impl is also what *exercises* (and thereby validates) the seam ADR 0010 describes.

**Phased:**

1. **User-scoped PATs first.** A key belongs to a user and inherits that user's per-suite grants → **no new authz model** (reuse the `view < edit < admin < owner` ladder; optionally down-scope a key to read-only — ideal for MCP read tools / dashboards, smallest blast radius). ~80% of the value for a fraction of the change.
2. **Service-account principals later.** These force the larger move and should be sequenced after PATs prove the seam: generalize `users.aad_object_id` into a generic **principal** with pluggable identity bindings (Azure AD oid, API key, …), and extend suite **sharing** to non-user principals.

**Credential bar (this is a new secret surface — not to be half-built):**

- Hash-at-rest (argon2/bcrypt), **show-once** on creation, identifying prefix (`dq_live_…`), expiry defaults, revocation, last-used + audit.
- Hashes live in a **new `api_keys` table** — *not* the credential SecretStore (that store is for *retrievable* connection secrets; a key hash is a verifier secret, never retrievable).
- **Lifecycle tied to the owner**: deactivating a user kills their keys, so a PAT can't become an SSO-policy backdoor that outlives the account.
- Never logged (prefix only) — same discipline as the compliance posture (#436).

## Consequences (anticipated)

- Discharges the identity-seam lock-in (ADR 0010/0013): Azure AD becomes *one* authenticator behind the seam, not the only one — unblocking BYOL on non-Azure clouds.
- Closes the programmatic-access gap for REST automation and headless MCP clients without weakening the per-user authz model (PAT phase).
- Adds a credential-management surface (issue/list/revoke UI + API) and an audit obligation.

## Open questions (for the full ADR)
